blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
By the Manifest Book: (2)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٣)
We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason, (3)
وَإِنَّهُۥ فِىٓ أُمِّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ (٤)
and it is sublime and wise with Us in the Mother Book. (4)
أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنكُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَ صَفْحًا أَن كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّسْرِفِينَ (٥)
Shall We keep back the Reminder from you and disregard you because you are an unrestrained lot? (5)
وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن نَّبِىٍّ فِى ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦)
How many a prophet We have sent to the former peoples! (6)
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٧)
There did not come to them any prophet but that they used to deride him. (7)
فَأَهْلَكْنَآ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا وَمَضَىٰ مَثَلُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨)
So We destroyed those who were stronger than these, and the example of the former peoples has already come to pass. (8)

Content of Surah Az-Zukhruf

Surah Az-Zukhruf is among the Meccan Surahs. Only regarding verse 45 of this Surah have a group of commentators deliberated, considering it Medinan—perhaps because its discussion pertains more to the People of the Book, or relates to the story of the Mi'raj (Ascension), either of which would be consistent with "Medina." God willing, we will clarify this matter in the commentary of that verse.

In any case, the nature of Meccan Surahs, which mostly revolve around the fundamental Islamic beliefs and discuss the Origin (Mabda'), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), Prophethood, the Quran, warnings, and glad tidings, is reflected within it.

The topics of this Surah can be briefly summarized into seven sections:

Section One: The beginning of the Surah, which speaks of the importance of the Magnificent Quran and the Prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and the undesirable reaction of ignorant individuals toward this Heavenly Book.

Section Two: It enumerates a portion of the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) in the "horizons" (the external world) and the various blessings of Allah upon humanity.

Section Three: It completes this same truth through the struggle against polytheism (Shirk), the negation of improper attributions to Allah, and the fight against blind imitation and superstitions—such as the aversion to daughters or the imagining of angels as the daughters of God.

Section Four: To visualize these truths, it narrates a portion of the history of previous Prophets and their peoples, placing particular emphasis on the lives of Ibrahim عليه السلام, Musa عليه السلام, and 'Isa عليه السلام.

Section Five: The issue of the Resurrection, the reward of the believers, and the grim fate of the disbelievers are raised, warning the criminals with powerful threats and admonitions.

Section Six: This is one of the most important sections of the Surah, observing the false values that governed—and still govern—the thoughts of faithless individuals. Because of these baseless values, they fall into various errors in their evaluation of important life matters, to the extent that they expect the Magnificent Quran to have been revealed to a wealthy man, for they considered personality to reside in wealth. In numerous verses of this Surah, the Magnificent Quran crushes this foolish thinking and defines the lofty Islamic and human values.

Section Seven: Found in most Surahs, this section consists of effective and fruitful exhortations and advice to complete the other sections, so that the collection of the Surah's verses forms a complete healing potion and exerts the strongest influence on the listener.

The name of the Surah is taken from verse 35, which speaks of material values and "Zukhruf" (gold and the like).

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In Islamic traditions across various books of commentary and Hadith, great virtue is mentioned for reciting this Surah. Among them, we read in a tradition from the Prophet of Islam:

"Whoever recites Surah Az-Zukhruf will be among those to whom it is said on the Day of Resurrection: 'O My servants! No fear shall be upon you this Day, nor shall you grieve. Enter Paradise without account.'" [1]

Of course, the address "O My servants! No fear shall be upon you this Day, nor shall you grieve" is exactly what appears in verse 68 of this Surah, and the phrase "Enter Paradise" is taken from verse 70, while the phrase "without account" is an implication of the discourse and other verses of the Quran.

In any case, this great glad tiding and boundless virtue are not achieved through a recitation devoid of reflection, faith, and action; for recitation is a prelude to reflection, and "faith" and "action" are the fruits thereof.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Az-Zukhruf.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ Ha, Meem (1). By the clear Book (2). Indeed, We have made it an Arabic Quran that you might understand (3). And indeed it is, in the Mother of the Book with Us, exalted and full of wisdom (4). Should We then take away the Reminder from you, disregarding you because you are a transgressing people? (5). And how many a prophet did We send among the former peoples (6). And there came not to them any prophet except that they used to ridicule him (7). So We destroyed those superior to them in power, and the example of the former peoples has passed (8).

Commentary

Your Sin Does Not Block Our Mercy!

At the beginning of this Surah, we again encounter the "Broken Letters" (Ha, Meem). This is the fourth Surah starting with "Ha, Meem"; three other Surahs also begin with these two letters, collectively forming the seven Surahs of the "Family of Ha Meem," which are in order: Al-Mu'min (Ghafir), Fussilat, Ash-Shura, Az-Zukhruf, Ad-Dukhan, Al-Jathiyah, and Al-Ahqaf.

Regarding the "Broken Letters," we have previously discussed them in detail (please refer to Volume 1 at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, Volume 2 at the beginning of Al-Imran, Volume 6 at the beginning of Al-A'raf, and Volume 20 at the beginning of Surah Fussilat regarding Ha Meem).

In the second verse, He swears by the Magnificent Quran, saying: "By this clear Book" (وَ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ). An oath by this Book whose truths are manifest, its concepts are clear, its proofs of truthfulness are evident, and its paths of guidance are plain and clarifying.

That "Indeed, We have made it an Arabic Quran that you might understand" (إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) [1]. The Quran being "Arabic" either means its revelation in the Arabic language, which is among the most expansive languages of the world for expressing truths and can beautifully reflect the subtleties of matters with complete elegance; or it means its "eloquence" (as one of the meanings of Arabi is "eloquent"), indicating that He placed it in the ultimate state of eloquence so that fine truths would appear from between its words and sentences, and everyone might understand it well.

It is interesting that here the oath and the response to the oath are both the same thing. He swears by the Quran that this Book has been made Arabic so that everyone may realize its content. Perhaps this indicates that there was nothing loftier than the Quran to be sworn by; loftier than the Quran is the Quran itself, for it is the Word of Allah, and the Word of Allah manifests His Pure Essence.

The expression "La'alla" (perhaps, or in the hope that...) is not because Allah has any doubt in the Quran's influence, nor is it a matter of a hope or wish that is difficult to attain. No, this expression refers to the differences in the intellectual and moral backgrounds of the listeners of the Quranic verses, and indicates that the influence of the Quran has conditions, which are briefly alluded to by the word "La'alla" (a further explanation of this meaning was given in Volume 3 under verse 200 of Al-Imran).

[1]: The Waw in "Wal-Kitab al-Mubin" is for the oath, and the response to this oath is the sentence "Inna ja'alnahu Qur'anan Arabiyya."

Then, He proceeds to describe three other attributes of this Heavenly Book, saying: "And indeed it is, in the Mother of the Book, in the Preserved Tablet with Us, exalted and full of wisdom" (وَ إِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ). In the first description, He points out that the Magnificent Quran is recorded and preserved in the "Mother of the Book" (Umm al-Kitab) with the Lord, just as we read in verse 22 of Surah Al-Buruj: "But this is a honored Quran, [inscribed] in a Preserved Tablet."

Now let us see what is meant by "Umm al-Kitab" or the "Preserved Tablet"? The word "Umm" (Mother) linguistically means the origin and foundation of anything. The reason Arabs call a mother "Umm" is that she is the root of the family and the refuge for children. Therefore, "Umm al-Kitab" (The Mother Book) means the book that is the origin and foundation of all Heavenly Books, and it is that same Tablet which is preserved with Allah from any change, alteration, or distortion. This is the very "Book of the Lord's Knowledge" which is with Him, and all the truths of the universe, all future and past events, and all Heavenly Books are inscribed in it, and no one has access to it except what Allah wills to disclose.

This is a great description for the Quran—that it originates from the infinite Knowledge of the Truth and its origin and foundation are with Him. For this reason, in the second description, He says: "This is an exalted Book" (La'ali). And in the third description, He says: "It is wisdom-teaching, firm, solid, and calculated" (Hakim).

Something that originates from the infinite Knowledge of the Truth must possess these attributes. Some have considered the "exaltation" and high status of the Quran to be because it has surpassed all previous Heavenly Books, abrogated them all, and is at the highest level of miracle. Others have considered the Quran's inclusion of truths that are beyond the reach of human thought (in addition to the truths that everyone understands from its literal meaning) as another concept of the Quran's exaltation. These concepts do not contradict each other, and all of them are gathered in the concept of "Ali" (Exalted).

It is also noteworthy that "Hakim" (Wise/Firm) is usually an attribute for a person, not a book, but since this Heavenly Book is itself a great teacher and a source of wisdom, this expression is very appropriate for it. Of course, "Hakim" also means firm and impenetrable, and all these concepts are gathered in the said word and are true regarding the Quran, for the Quran is "Hakim" in all these senses.

In the next verse, He addresses the deniers and those who turn away from the Quran, saying: "Should We then take away this Quran—which is the cause of your awakening and reminder—from you because you are a transgressing and extremist people?!" (أَ فَنَضْرِبُ عَنْكُمُ الذِّكْرَ صَفْحاً أَنْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُسْرِفِينَ). It is true that you have spared nothing in your enmity and opposition to the truth, and have taken opposition to the level of excess and transgression, but the grace and mercy of Allah are so vast and expansive that He does not see these as obstacles in His path. He continues to reveal this awakening Heavenly Book and its life-giving verses upon you, so that hearts with even a slight readiness might be shaken and find the way. This is the station of General Mercy and the "Rahmaniyyah" of the Lord, which encompasses both friend and foe.

The phrase "A-fanadribu 'ankum" means "Should We then turn away from you and divert [it]?" because when a rider wants to turn his mount from one path to another, he strikes it with a whip; hence the word "Darb" (striking) is used in such cases instead of "Sarf" (diverting) [1]. "Safh" originally means the side or edge of something, and it also means width and breadth; in the verse under discussion, it carries the first meaning—that is, should We incline this Quran, which is a source of reminder, away from you toward another direction?

"Musrif" is from the root "Israf," meaning exceeding the limit, indicating that the polytheists and enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recognized no boundaries or limits in their opposition and enmity.

Then, as a witness and testimony to what was said, and as a consolation and comfort for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and simultaneously a threat to the stubborn deniers, He says in a short and firm phrase: "And how many a prophet did We send for guidance among the former peoples" (وَ كَمْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ نَبِيٍّ فِي الْأَوَّلِينَ).

"But no prophet came to them except that they used to ridicule and mock him" (وَ ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ). These oppositions and mockeries never prevented Divine Grace; this is a bounty that has continued from eternity to eternity, an existence He bestows upon all servants, and He created them for mercy in the first place: "And for that He created them" (Hud-119).

For this reason, your turning away and stubbornness will never prevent His Grace, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the true believers should not be discouraged, for this turning away from the truth and following of lusts and desires also has a long history!

However, so they do not imagine that Allah's boundless Grace will prevent their punishment at the end of the matter—for punishment itself is a requirement of His Wisdom—He adds in the next verse: "We destroyed those who were stronger than these in power" (فَأَهْلَكْنا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ بَطْشاً). "And the example of the former peoples has already passed" (وَ مَضى مَثَلُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

In the verses We have previously revealed to you, many examples of these rebellious and tyrannical peoples have been raised, and their life stories have been revealed to you through revelation without any omission. Among them were peoples much stronger than the Arab polytheists, with vast resources, wealth, numbers, armies, and talents—peoples like Pharaoh and his folk, and powerful ones like the people of 'Ad and Thamud. But go and see the ruins of their cities, read their stories in history, and most clearly of all, examine what has been revealed in the Quran about them, so you may know that you stubborn tyrants are never safe from the painful Divine punishment.

"Batsh," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means "seizing something with power," and here it is accompanied by the word "Ashadd" (stronger), which is a sign of even greater power and force.

The pronoun in "minhum" (than them) refers back to the Arab polytheists who were addressed in the previous verses, but here they are mentioned in the third person (absent), as they are not worthy of the continuation of the Divine address.

Some great commentators have considered the phrase "And the example of the former peoples has passed" to refer to the matters mentioned in the previous Surah (Surah Ash-Shura) regarding a group of them. However, there is no evidence for this limitation, especially since in Surah Ash-Shura there is very little mention of the history of former peoples, while in other Surahs of the Quran, there are detailed discussions about them.

In any case, this verse is similar to what appeared in verse 78 of Surah Al-Qasas: "Did he [Qarun] not know that Allah had destroyed before him of generations those who were greater than him in power and greater in accumulation?" Or what appeared in verse 21 of Surah Al-Mu'min (Ghafir), where He warns the Arab polytheists, saying: "Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those who were before them? They were greater than them in power and in impressions [left] on the land; but Allah seized them for their sins, and they had not from Allah any protector."

Ayahs

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٩)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘The All-mighty, the All-knowing created them.’ (9)
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٠)
He, who made the earth a cradle for you, and made for you in it ways so that you may be guided [to your destinations], (10)
وَٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنشَرْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ (١١)
and who sent down water from the sky in a measured manner, and We revived with it a dead country. Likewise, you shall be raised [from the dead]. (11)
وَٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْفُلْكِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ مَا تَرْكَبُونَ (١٢)
He created all the kinds and made for you the ships and the cattle such which you ride, (12)
لِتَسْتَوُۥا۟ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِۦ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُۥ مُقْرِنِينَ (١٣)
that you may sit on their backs, then remember the blessing of your Lord when you are settled on them, and say, ‘Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us, and we [by ourselves] were no match for it. (13)
وَإِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ (١٤)
Indeed we shall return to our Lord.’ (14)

Commentary

A Portion of the Proofs of Monotheism

From here, the discussion regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and Polytheism (Shirk) begins. First, it draws upon human nature (Fitrah) and innate disposition to prove Monotheism. Then, it utilizes the evidence present in the system of the universe, and while mentioning five examples of the Lord's blessings, it stirs their sense of gratitude. Subsequently, it proceeds to invalidate their superstitious beliefs regarding idols and various forms of polytheism.

In the first part, He says: "And if you ask them who created the heavens and the earth, they will surely say, 'The All-Mighty, the All-Knowing created them'" (وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ).

This expression, which appears in four verses of the Holy Quran with slight variations (Surah Al-Ankabut verse 61, Surah Luqman verse 25, Surah Az-Zumar verse 38, and the verse under discussion in Surah Az-Zukhruf) [^1], serves on one hand as proof of the innate nature of God-consciousness and the manifestation of Divine light within human disposition.

On the other hand, it is evidence that the polytheists confessed to the truth that the Creator of the heavens and the earth is Allah, and except in rare cases, they did not attribute the power of creation to their own deities.

Thirdly, this confession serves as a foundation for invalidating the worship of idols; for the one worthy of worship is the Creator and Disposer of the universe, not beings who have no share in this regard. Therefore, their confession of the creative power of "Allah" was a crushing blow to the validity of their corrupt religion.

The description "All-Mighty and All-Knowing" (Al-Aziz al-Alim), which expresses the absolute power of the Lord and His knowledge and wisdom, is indeed a Quranic expression, yet it was not something the polytheists denied. This is because the prerequisite for confessing to Allah's creation of the heavens and the earth is the existence of these two attributes in God. They even attributed knowledge and power to their idols, let alone to Allah, whom they considered the idols to be intermediaries between themselves and Him.

Then, He points to five categories of God's great blessings, each of which is an example of the system of creation and a sign among the signs of God. He begins first with the earth, saying: "[He] who has made for you the earth a cradle [and a place of rest]" (الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا).

The words mahd and mihad both mean a place prepared for sitting, sleeping,

[^1]: In two other instances in the Quran, their confession to "Allah's creativity" is also narrated; however, in one case it concerns the sending down of rain from the sky (Al-Ankabut: 63) and in the other, it concerns Allah's creation of themselves (Az-Zukhruf: 87).

and resting. Originally, it refers to the place where a child is laid to sleep, whether it be a cradle or otherwise.

Indeed, Allah made the earth a cradle for man. While it possesses several types of motion, under the light of the law of gravity and the vast layer of air surrounding it from every side, along with various other factors, it is so calm that its inhabitants feel the slightest discomfort. We know that the blessing of tranquility and security is the primary foundation for benefiting from other blessings. It is obvious that if these various factors did not work together, this tranquility would never have existed.

To describe the second blessing, He adds: "And made for you therein ways that you may be guided [and reach your destination]" (وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ).

This blessing, which has been mentioned several times in the Holy Quran (Surah Ta-Ha verse 53, Surah Al-Anbiya verse 31, and Surah An-Nahl verse 15), is among the blessings that many are heedless of. We know that almost all landmasses are covered by numerous folds, large and small mountains, and various hills. Interestingly, within the world's largest mountain ranges, there are often gaps through which man can find his way. It rarely happens that these mountains completely separate different parts of the earth. This is one of the secrets of the system of creation and one of the Divine favors upon His servants.

Furthermore, many parts of the earth are connected to one another via sea routes, which is also included in the general meaning of the verse. [^1]

[^1]: The word subul (plural of sabil) applies to both land routes and sea routes, as we read in section 42 of the Jawshan Dua: "O He whose path is in the land and the sea" (يا من فى البر و البحر سبيله).

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the phrase "that you may be guided" (la'allakum tahtadun) refers to being guided to one's destination and finding different regions of the earth, although some have interpreted it as a reference to being guided in the matter of Monotheism and God-consciousness (of course, combining both meanings is also permissible).

The third favor, which is the issue of the descent of rainwater and the revival of dead lands, is presented in the following verse as follows: "[He] who sends down water from the sky in [due] measure" (وَالَّذِي نَزَّلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً بِقَدَرٍ). "And We give life thereby to a dead land" (فَأَنْشَرْنَا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتًا). "Even so will you be brought forth [from your graves after death]" (كَذَلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ).

The expression "in measure" (bi-qadarin) is a subtle reference to the specific system governing the descent of rain; it falls in an amount that is beneficial and productive, not harmful.

It is true that sometimes floods occur and destroy lands, but these are exceptional cases and serve as warnings. However, the vast majority of rains are advantageous, useful, and profitable. Fundamentally, the cultivation of all trees, plants, flowers, and productive farms is by the grace of this measured descent of rain. If the descent of rain had no system, all these blessings would not be attained.

In the second part of the verse, emphasis is placed on the word ansharmā, derived from nushūr meaning "to spread out," which visualizes the resurrection of the plant world: parched lands that have hidden the seeds of plants like the corpses of the dead in graves within their depths, are set in motion by the "blast of the trumpet" of the falling rain. They stir, and the plant-dead raise their heads from the soil, and a gathering (mahshar) is established which is itself an example

of the resurrection of humans, which is referred to at the end of this very verse and in numerous other verses of the Holy Quran.

In the fourth stage, after mentioning the descent of rain and the life of plants, He refers to the creation of various animals, saying: "[He] who created all pairs" (وَالَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْوَاجَ كُلَّهَا). The expression "pairs" (azwāj) is a metaphor for the various types of animals, based on the context of the plants mentioned in the previous verses, although some commentators have taken it as a reference to all types of beings, including animals, plants, and minerals, since the law of pairing governs them all, and each has an opposite: heaven and earth, night and day, light and darkness, salty and sweet, dry and wet, sun and moon, Paradise and Hell—except for the Essence of Allah, the Pure, who is One and Unique, and no duality finds its way into His Holy Essence.

However, as we have said, the existing context indicates that "pairs of animals" is intended. We know that the law of pairing is the law of life in all living creatures, and rare or exceptional cases do not prevent the law from being universal.

Some have also taken azwāj to mean the classes of animals, such as birds, quadrupeds, aquatic creatures, insects, and others.

In the fifth stage, which describes the final blessing in this series, the talk is of the mounts that Allah has placed at the disposal of mankind for traversing sea and land routes. He says: "And has made for you of ships and cattle those which you ride" (وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعَامِ مَا تَرْكَبُونَ).

This is one of the favors and honors of Allah toward the human race, which is not seen in

other types of living beings—that Allah has carried man upon mounts that assist him in journeys across the sea and the desert.

As stated in verse 70 of Surah Al-Isra: "And We have certainly honored the children of Adam and carried them on the land and sea and provided for them of the good things and preferred them over much of what We have created, with [definite] preference" (وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِي آدَمَ وَحَمَلْنَاهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَرَزَقْنَاهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَفَضَّلْنَاهُمْ عَلَى كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا).

Truly, the existence of these mounts multiplies human activity and the expansion of his life several times over. Even today's high-speed vehicles, placed at man's disposal by utilizing the properties of various elements, are among the manifest graces of God—means that have completely transformed the face of his life, accelerated everything, and brought him all kinds of comfort.

The next verse explains the ultimate goal of creating these mounts as follows: "That you may settle yourselves upon their backs and then remember the favor of your Lord when you have settled upon them and say, 'Exalted is He who has subjected this to us, and we could not have [otherwise] subdued it'" (لِتَسْتَوُوا عَلَى ظُهُورِهِ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا اسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ). The phrase "that you may settle yourselves upon their backs" indicates that He created these mounts in such a way that you can properly ride them and reach your destination with ease. [^1]

[^1]: The pronoun in "upon its back" (ala zuhurih) refers back to the relative pronoun ma (that which) in the phrase "that which you ride" (ma tarkabun), which includes both ships and cattle; its singular form is due to the literal appearance of the word.

In this verse, two goals are stated for the creation of these sea and desert mounts: first, remembering the favors of the Lord when established upon them, and second, glorifying God who has subjected these to the command of man. He created ships such that they can cleave through the waves and move toward the destination, and He made the cattle tame and submissive before man.

Muqrinīn is derived from the root iqrān, meaning to have power and ability over something. Some lexicographers have also said it means "to control" and "to keep" something. Originally, it meant to be the "companion" (qarin) of something, the prerequisite of which is the ability to keep and control it. [^1] Therefore, the phrase "and we could not have subdued it" (wa ma kunna lahu muqrinin) means that if it were not for the grace of the Lord and His favors, we would never have had the ability to control and keep these mounts. Contrary winds would have constantly overturned the ships and prevented us from reaching the shore of safety. And these powerful animals, whose strength is many times greater than man's—if the spirit of submission did not govern them, man could never even approach them. For this reason, occasionally when one of these animals becomes angry and loses the spirit of submission, they turn into dangerous creatures that several people do not have the power to confront. Yet, in a normal state, dozens or hundreds of them may be tied to a rope and handed to a child to "lead wherever he desires."

It is as if God, through these exceptional states of the cattle, wishes to clarify the blessing of their normal state.

[^1]: In Lisan al-Arab it is stated: "aqrana lahu wa alayh": he was capable of it, strong over it, and overcame it. And in the Mighty Revelation: "and we were not muqrinin to it," meaning capable.

In the last verse under discussion, He completes the speech of the true believers when mounting a vehicle in this way: "And indeed, to our Lord we will return" (وَإِنَّا إِلَى رَبِّنَا لَمُنْقَلِبُونَ).

This sentence is a reference to the issue of the Resurrection after the discussions regarding Monotheism in these verses, because attention to the Creator and the Origin always directs man toward the Resurrection as well.

It is also an indication that you should not become arrogant when mounting and gaining mastery over these swift vehicles, nor should you become immersed in the glitz and glamour of the world. You must, in any case, remember the Hereafter, for the state of arrogance occurs frequently, especially at such times, and those who use their mounts as a means of seeking superiority and pride over others are not few.

Thirdly, mounting a vehicle and moving from one place to another draws our attention to our great transition from this world to the next. Yes, we ultimately go toward God.

Key Point: Remembering God When Utilizing Blessings

One of the interesting points seen in the verses of the Quran is that it teaches believers prayers to recite when utilizing Divine favors—prayers whose constructive content builds the human soul and spirit and wipes away the effects of arrogance and heedlessness.

He commands Noah (عليه السلام): "And when you have boarded the ship, you and those with you, say, 'Praise be to Allah who has saved us from the wrongdoing people'" (Al-Mu'minun: 28).

He also commands him to say, when requesting to disembark at a blessed destination: "My Lord, let me land at a blessed landing place, and You are the best of those who bring to land" (Al-Mu'minun: 29).

And in the verses under discussion, we read that He gives the command to thank the Lord for His blessings and to glorify Him when being positioned upon mounts.

Whenever this becomes a human habit—to remember the True Benefactor and the source of that blessing when utilizing any favor—he will neither sink into the darkness of heedlessness nor fall into the abyss of arrogance. Rather, material favors become a bridge for him toward God!

It is narrated regarding the states of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) that whenever he placed his foot in the stirrup, he would say: "In the name of Allah" (Bismillah), and when he was settled upon the mount, he would say: "Praise be to Allah in every state. Exalted is He who has subjected this to us, and we could not have [otherwise] subdued it, and indeed, to our Lord we will return." [^1]

In another narration from Imam al-Mujtaba, Hasan ibn Ali (عليه السلام), it is mentioned that a man in his presence, while mounting a vehicle, said: "Exalted is He who has subjected this to us." The Imam said: "You have not been commanded thus. The command is that you say: 'Praise be to Allah who guided us to Islam; praise be to Allah who favored us with Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله); and praise be to Allah who made us among the best nation brought forth for mankind.' Then you say: 'Exalted is He who has subjected this to us.'" ^2

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, vol. 27, p. 199.

This indicates that in the verse, the command is not merely to say "Exalted is He who has subjected this to us," but rather, the command was previously given to remember and recall the greater blessings of God: the blessing of guidance toward Islam, the blessing of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) prophethood, the blessing of being among the best of nations, and then glorifying God for the subjection of this mount!

It is noteworthy that it is understood from some narrations that whoever says this phrase (Subhanalladhi sakhkhara lana hadha...) when mounting a vehicle, by God's command, no harm shall reach him!

This matter is narrated in a hadith in the book Al-Kafi from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام). [^1] How great is the difference between these constructive teachings of Islam and what is seen from a group of self-indulgent and arrogant people who make their vehicles a means of showing off and boasting, and sometimes a means for various sins. As Zamakhshari narrates in Al-Kashshaf regarding one of the sultans, he had mounted his special vehicle and was traveling from one city to another, with a month's distance between them. He drank so much wine that he never noticed the journey; he only regained consciousness from his intoxication when he had reached the destination!

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 593.

Ayahs

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ جُزْءًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
They ascribe to Him offspring from among His servants! Man is indeed a manifest ingrate. (15)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَنَاتٍ وَأَصْفَىٰكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ (١٦)
Did He adopt daughters from what He creates while He preferred you with sons? (16)
وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِمَا ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مَثَلًا ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ (١٧)
When one of them is brought the news of what he ascribes to the All-beneficent, his face becomes darkened and he chokes with suppressed rage, [and says] (17)
أَوَمَن يُنَشَّؤُا۟ فِى ٱلْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْخِصَامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ (١٨)
‘What! One who is brought up amid ornaments and is inconspicuous in contests?’ (18)
وَجَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبَـٰدُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ أَشَهِدُوا۟ خَلْقَهُمْ ۚ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهَـٰدَتُهُمْ وَيُسْـَٔلُونَ (١٩)
They have made the angels—who are servants of the All-beneficent—females. Were they witness to their creation? Their testimony will be written down and they shall be questioned. (19)

Commentary

How Can You Call the Angels Daughters of Allah?!

After strengthening the foundations of Monotheism (Tawhid) by enumerating the signs of Allah in the system of existence and His blessings and bounties, the verses under discussion address the opposite point—namely, the struggle against polytheism (Shirk) and the worship of other than Allah. First, it addresses one of its branches, which is the worship of angels, stating: "And they attribute to Him a portion of His servants" (وَ جَعَلُوا لَهُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ جُزْءاً).

They imagined the angels to be the daughters of God and their own deities—an ugly superstition that was prevalent among many idolaters.

The expression "portion" (juz') indicates that they considered the angels to be the children of God, because a child is always a portion of the existence of the parents, separating from them in the form of a germ-cell, combining, and forming the nucleus of the child's development.

Furthermore, it expresses the acceptance of their divinity, as they imagined the angels to be a "portion" of deities alongside Allah.

This expression simultaneously serves as a clear logical argument for the invalidity of the polytheists' superstitious belief; for if the angels were children of God, it would necessitate that Allah has parts (portions), resulting in the composition (tarkib) of the Pure Essence of God. This is while intellectual and traditional proofs testify to the simplicity (basatat) and absolute Oneness (Ahadiyyah) of His existence, as having parts is a characteristic exclusive to contingent beings (possible existences).

Then He adds: "Indeed, man is clearly ungrateful" (إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَكَفُورٌ مُبِينٌ).

All these divine blessings encompass his entire being—five categories of which were mentioned in the preceding verses—yet instead of prostrating at the threshold of his Creator and Benefactor, he chooses the path of ingratitude and turns toward His creatures.

In the next verse, to condemn this superstitious thinking, He utilizes their own mentalities and accepted norms. Since they preferred the male gender over the female and generally considered a daughter to be a source of shame, He says: "Has He taken daughters from what He creates and chosen sons for you?!" (أَمِ اتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَناتٍ وَ أَصْفاكُمْ بِالْبَنِينَ).

In your imagination, the status of a daughter is lower; how then do you prefer yourselves over God? You assign the share of daughters to Him and the share of sons to yourselves?! It is true that men and women are equal in the sight of Allah regarding high human values, but sometimes arguing based on the audience's own mentalities has an impact on their thinking that forces them to reconsider.

He pursues this same point with another expression, saying: "And when one of them is given the glad tidings of that which he attributes as a likeness to the Most Merciful, his face turns black [with distress] and he is filled with suppressed rage!" (وَ إِذا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُمْ بِما ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمنِ مَثَلًا ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُ مُسْوَدًّا وَ هُوَ كَظِيمٌ).

The meaning of "that which he attributes as a likeness to the Most Merciful" refers to the angels whom they considered daughters of God, while simultaneously making them their deities and His likeness! The word "Kazim" is derived from "Kazm" (rhyming with Nazm), meaning the throat; it is also used for tying the neck of a water-skin after it is filled. Therefore, this word is used for someone whose heart is filled with rage, grief, or sorrow.

This expression vividly reflects the superstitious thinking of the foolish polytheists in the Age of Ignorance regarding the birth of a female child—how they would be distressed upon hearing the news of a daughter's birth, yet they considered the angels to be the daughters of God!

Continuing this discourse, He adds: "Is one who is brought up among ornaments and who, in dispute, cannot make a clear case [to be attributed to God, while you take sons for yourselves]?" (أَ وَ مَنْ يُنَشَّؤُا فِي الْحِلْيَةِ وَ هُوَ فِي الْخِصامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ) [1]. Here, the Quran discusses two characteristics of women that are seen in most of them and stem from their emotional nature: first, their strong inclination toward ornaments, and second, the lack of sufficient power to prove their point during disputes and arguments due to modesty and shyness.

Undoubtedly, there are women who have little inclination toward ornaments, and undoubtedly, an interest in ornaments "in moderation" is not considered a flaw for women;

[1]: "Yunashsha'u" is from the root "Nasha'", meaning to bring something into existence, and here it means to be raised or nurtured. "Hilyah" means ornament, and "Khisam" means dispute, argument, and struggling over something.

Rather, it has been emphasized in Islam. The reference is to the majority who, in most human societies, have a habit of excessive ornamentation, as if they come into being and are nurtured amidst jewelry.

Similarly, there are undoubtedly individuals among women who are very strong in terms of logic and eloquence, but it cannot be denied that the majority of them, due to modesty and shyness, have less power during disputes and arguments compared to men.

The goal is to express this reality: how can you imagine daughters to be the children of God while considering sons for yourselves?!

In the final verse under discussion, He presents the matter with even greater clarity, saying: "And they made the angels, who are servants of the Most Merciful, females" (and introduced them as daughters of God) (وَ جَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً).

Yes, they are the servants of Allah; they are obedient to His command and surrendered to His will, as stated in verses 26 and 27 of Surah Al-Anbiya (and Surah Al-Hajj): "Nay, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command."

The use of the term "Ibad" (servants) is actually an answer to their imagination; for if they were female, the term "Ibadat" should have been used. However, it should be noted that "Ibad" is a masculine plural, and it is also applied to beings that are outside the categories of male and female, such as angels—just as masculine singular pronouns are used for Allah, while He is above all these categories.

It is noteworthy that in this sentence, "Ibad" is annexed to "Ar-Rahman" (The Most Merciful). This expression may indicate that most angels are the executors of Allah's mercy and the managers of the systems of the universe, which is entirely mercy.

But why did this superstition arise among the pagan Arabs? And why do its remnants still remain in the minds of some today, to the extent that they depict angels as women and girls? Even when they visualize the so-called "Angel of Liberty," they depict her in the face of a woman with specific features and long hair! This notion might stem from the fact that angels are hidden from sight, and women were also mostly hidden (veiled). This meaning is even seen in some metaphorical feminines in the Arabic language, where the sun is considered metaphorically feminine and the moon masculine—because the sun's disk is usually hidden within its waves of light and cannot be easily looked at, whereas the moon's disk is not so.

Or perhaps the refinement (latafat) of the angels' existence caused them to be considered of the same kind as women, who are more refined beings compared to men. Surprisingly, after all this struggle by Islam against this superstitious thinking, when people want to describe a woman's goodness, they still say "she is an angel," but this expression is rarely used for men, and the word "Fereshteh" (Angel) is also a name chosen for women!

Then, in the form of a rhetorical question to answer them, He says: "Were they present at their creation [to know this through their presence]?" (أَ شَهِدُوا خَلْقَهُمْ).

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "Their testimony [to this baseless belief] will be recorded [in their scrolls of deeds], and they will be questioned [on the Day of Resurrection]" (سَتُكْتَبُ شَهادَتُهُمْ وَ يُسْئَلُونَ).

What we read in the above verses also appears in another form in Surah An-Nahl (verses 57 to 59), where we have provided detailed discussions regarding the beliefs of the pagan Arabs concerning the issue of "Wa'd" (burying daughters alive), their general belief regarding the female gender, and the role of Islam in reviving the personality and value of the status of women (Volume 11, pages 269 to 277).

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَا عَبَدْنَـٰهُم ۗ مَّا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (٢٠)
They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They do not have any knowledge of that, and they do nothing but surmise. (20)
أَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ فَهُم بِهِۦ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ (٢١)
Did We give them a Book before this, so that they are holding fast to it? (21)
بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٢٢)
No, they said, ‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided in their footsteps.’ (22)

Commentary

Do They Have Any Proof Other Than Imitating Ignorant Ancestors?

In the preceding verses, the first logical response was given to the superstitious belief of the idolaters who imagined angels to be the daughters of Allah. That response was that to prove any claim, observation, sight, and presence at the scene are required above all else, while none of the idolaters can ever claim to have been witnesses or observers at the scene of the creation of the angels.

The verses under discussion follow up on this same meaning, proceeding to invalidate this ugly superstition through other means. First, it briefly cites one of their hollow arguments along with its rebuttal, stating: "And they said: 'If the Most Merciful had willed, we would never have worshipped them.'" They claimed it was His will that led them to worship them! (وَ قالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ).

This expression might mean that they believed in fatalism (determinism), claiming that whatever originates from them is by the will of Allah, and whatever action they perform is pleasing to Him.

Alternatively, it could mean that if our actions and beliefs were not pleasing to Him, He should have forbidden them, and since He did not forbid them, it is proof of His satisfaction! In reality, to justify their corrupt and superstitious beliefs, they resorted to other superstitions, weaving further lies for their false imaginings. However, whichever of the two aforementioned possibilities they intended, it is corrupt and baseless.

It is true that nothing occurs in the universe without the will of Allah, but this does not mean fatalism. For it must not be forgotten that Allah willed for us to be autonomous and possess freedom of will so that He may test us and nurture our development.

And it is true that Allah must critique the deeds of His servants, but it cannot be denied that all the Prophets of Allah rejected every form of polytheism and dualism.

Furthermore, the sound human intellect also rejects these superstitions. Is not "intellect" the Prophet of Allah within the human being?

At the end of this verse, He responds to this hollow reasoning of the idolaters with this short sentence: "They have no knowledge of such a thing [that they claim], and they do nothing but lie" (ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ).

They do not even have knowledge or faith regarding the issue of fatalism or Allah's satisfaction with their deeds. Rather, they are like many other ego-worshippers and criminals who, to exonerate themselves from sin and corruption, bring up the subject of fatalism and say: "The hand of destiny has led us down this path!" while they themselves know they are lying, and these are nothing more than excuses. Therefore, if someone were to trample upon their rights, they would never be willing to overlook his punishment on the grounds that he was compelled in his action! "Yakhruṣūn" is derived from the root "kharṣ," which originally means to estimate.

It was first used regarding the estimation of the amount of fruit on trees, then applied to any kind of guesswork and conjecture. Since guesswork and conjecture sometimes turn out to be incorrect, this word has also been used to mean lying, and it is of this type in the verse under discussion.

In any case, it appears from several verses of the Holy Quran that the idolaters repeatedly argued using the issue of Divine Will to justify their superstitious beliefs. Among these was that they had forbidden some things and permitted others for themselves, attributing this to Allah, as stated in Surah Al-An'am, verse 148: سَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما أَشْرَكْنا وَ لا آباؤُنا وَ لا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ: "Those who associated others with Allah will say, 'If Allah had willed, we would not have associated [anything] and neither would our fathers, nor would we have prohibited anything.'"

This same meaning is repeated in Surah An-Nahl, verse 35: وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ نَحْنُ وَ لا آباؤُنا وَ لا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ.

The Holy Quran refutes them at the end of the verse in Surah Al-An'am, saying: كَذلِكَ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ حَتَّى ذاقُوا بَأْسَنا: "In this way those before them denied until they tasted Our punishment." And at the end of the verse in Surah An-Nahl, it explicitly states: فَهَلْ عَلَى الرُّسُلِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ: "Then is there anything upon the messengers except clear notification?" And at the end of the verse under discussion, as we have seen, it attributes to them false estimation, all of which in reality return to a single root.

In the next verse, He points to another argument they might use, saying: "Or have We given them a book before this [Quran] to which they are adhering?" (أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً مِنْ قَبْلِهِ فَهُمْ بِهِ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ) [1]. That is, to prove this claim, they must adhere either to the proof of intellect or to transmitted evidence (scripture). Yet they have neither a proof from intellect nor a proof from scripture; all intellectual proofs call toward Monotheism (Tawhid), and all Prophets and Heavenly Books have likewise called toward Monotheism.

In the final verse under discussion, He points to their primary excuse, which is also in reality nothing more than a superstition that has become the foundation for another superstition. He says: "Rather, they say: 'Indeed, we found our forefathers upon a religion, and we are guided by their footsteps'" (بَلْ قالُوا إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى‌ أُمَّةٍ وَ إِنَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ مُهْتَدُونَ). In reality, they had no reason other than "blind imitation" of their fathers and ancestors.

[1]: "Am" here is muttaṣilah (conjunctive), and is coordinated with "أَ شَهِدُوا خَلْقَهُمْ" (Did they witness their creation?). The pronoun in "min qablihi" (before it) refers to the Quran. The possibility suggested by some that "am" is munqaṭi'ah (disjunctive) or that the pronoun refers to "the Messenger" does not align well with the context of the verse.

The strange thing is that they considered themselves guided through this imitation, whereas in matters of belief and intellectual foundations, no sensible and free-thinking person can rely on imitation—especially the imitation of "the ignorant by the ignorant." For we know that their ancestors also possessed no science or knowledge; their brains were filled with superstitions and delusions, and ignorance governed their thoughts and society, as the Quran says in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 170: أَ وَ لَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَهْتَدُونَ: "Even though their fathers understood nothing, nor were they guided?" Imitation is only correct in secondary and non-fundamental matters, and even then, it is the imitation of a scholar—meaning the recourse of the ignorant to the expert—just as is seen in a patient's recourse to a physician, or non-specialists to specialists. Therefore, their imitation was void and condemned for two reasons.

The word "Ummah," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, refers to a group of people who have some kind of connection to one another, whether in terms of religion, unity of place, or time, whether that connecting link is voluntary or involuntary. (For this reason, it is sometimes used to mean "religion" or "creed," as in the verse under discussion, but its original meaning is that very group or community, and applying this word to a religion requires contextual evidence) [1].

[1]: In the sentence "إِنَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ مُهْتَدُونَ," "muhtadūn" is the predicate (khabar) of "inna," and "ala atharihim" is related to it. The possibility suggested by some that "ala atharihim" is the first predicate and "muhtadūn" is the second predicate seems unlikely.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّقْتَدُونَ (٢٣)
So it has been that We did not send any warner to a town before you, without its affluent ones saying, ‘We found our fathers following a creed and we are indeed following in their footsteps.’ (23)
۞ قَـٰلَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُم بِأَهْدَىٰ مِمَّا وَجَدتُّمْ عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٢٤)
He would say, ‘What! Even if I bring you a better guidance than what you found your fathers following?!’ They would say, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you are sent with.’ (24)
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٢٥)
Thereupon We took vengeance on them; so observe how was the fate of the deniers. (25)

Commentary

The Fate of These Blind Followers!

These verses continue the discussion of the previous verses regarding the primary excuse of the polytheists for idol-worship, which was the issue of imitating their ancestors.

First, it states: This is not merely the claim of the Arab polytheists, "And similarly, We did not send before you any warner to a city but that its wealthy ones, intoxicated and arrogant, said: 'Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and we are following in their footsteps'" (وَ كَذلِكَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ إِلَّا قالَ مُتْرَفُوها إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى‌ أُمَّةٍ وَ إِنَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ مُقْتَدُونَ‌).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that the leaders of the struggle against the Prophets, and those who raised the issue of imitating ancestors and stood firmly upon it, were the "mutrafun"—the wealthy, intoxicated, arrogant, and affluent. This is because "mutraf" is derived from the root "turfah" (on the weight of luqmah), meaning an abundance of blessings. Since many of those blessed with abundance drown in desires and whims, the word mutraf has come to mean those who have become intoxicated and arrogant by blessings and have rebelled [^1]. Its manifestations are often kings, tyrants, and arrogant, selfish wealthy individuals. Indeed, it was they whose period of self-will would be ended by the uprising of the Prophets, whose illegitimate interests would be endangered, and from whose clutches the oppressed would be liberated. For this reason, they engaged in numbing and deceiving the people with various tricks and excuses. Even today, most of the world's corruption stems from these very "mutrafin," who are actively present wherever there is oppression, aggression, sin, and pollution.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verse we read that they said: "إِنَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ مُهْتَدُونَ‌" ("Indeed, we are guided by their footsteps"), while here it quotes them saying: "إِنَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ مُقْتَدُونَ‌" ("Indeed, we are following in their footsteps"). Although both expressions essentially return to the same meaning, the first expression refers to the claim of the validity of the ancestors' religion, while the second expression refers to their insistence and persistence in following and imitating their ancestors.

In any case, this verse serves as a kind of consolation for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers, so they may know that the excuse-making of the polytheists is nothing new; this is the same path that all the misguided have traversed throughout history!

The next verse clearly states the response that the previous Prophets gave to them, saying: "Their Prophet said to them: 'Even if I brought you a religion clearer and more guiding than that upon which you found your fathers, would you still deny it?'" (قالَ أَ وَ لَوْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِأَهْدى‌ مِمَّا وَجَدْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ آباءَكُمْ‌) [^1]. This is the most polite expression that can be stated to a stubborn and arrogant people so that their emotions are not wounded in any way. He does not say that what you have is a lie, a superstition, or stupidity; rather, he says: "What I have brought is more guiding than the religion of your ancestors; come, look, and study." Such Quranic expressions teach us etiquette in discussion and debate, especially when facing the arrogant ignorant.

Yet, despite all this, they were so drowned in ignorance, prejudice, and stubbornness that even this calculated and polite speech had no effect on them. In response to their Prophets, they only "said: 'Indeed, we are disbelievers in that with which you have been sent!'" (قالُوا إِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ‌).

They said this without bringing the slightest reason for their opposition, and without giving the slightest thought or reflection to the sound proposal of the Prophets and Divine Messengers.

It is obvious that such a rebellious, stubborn, and illogical people do not deserve to remain and live, and sooner or later, Divine punishment must descend to remove these thorns and debris from the path. Therefore, in the last verse under discussion, He says: "So We took retribution from them and punished them severely" (فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ‌). One group was destroyed by a flood, another by a devastating earthquake, and another by a violent wind and lightning; in short, We crushed and destroyed each of them with a crushing command.

At the end of the verse, so that the polytheists of Mecca may also take heed, He addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "So see how was the end of the deniers!" (فانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ‌).

The assembly of the stubborn polytheists of Mecca must also await such a grim fate!

[^1]: In "Lisan al-Arab" we read: atrafat-hu al-ni'mah, meaning it made him rebellious. [^1]: This sentence has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "Do you follow your fathers even if I brought you a religion more guiding than the religion of your fathers?" (Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Maraghi, Qurtubi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani).

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّنِى بَرَآءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ (٢٦)
When Abraham said to his father and his people, ‘I repudiate what you worship, (26)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ سَيَهْدِينِ (٢٧)
excepting Him who originated me; indeed He will guide me.’ (27)
وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةًۢ بَاقِيَةً فِى عَقِبِهِۦ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٢٨)
He made it a lasting word among his posterity so that they may come back [to the right path]. (28)
بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ وَرَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢٩)
Indeed, I provided for these and their fathers until the truth and a manifest apostle came to them. (29)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ وَإِنَّا بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٠)
But when the truth came to them, they said, ‘This is magic, and we indeed disbelieve in it.’ (30)

Commentary

Monotheism, the Eternal Word of the Prophets

In these verses, a brief reference is made to the story of Abraham (peace be upon him) and his encounter with the idol-worshipping people of Babylon. This serves to complete the discussion regarding the condemnation of blind imitation mentioned in the preceding verses. This is because, firstly, Abraham (peace be upon him) was the greatest ancestor of the Arabs; everyone respected him and took pride in his history. Since he tore through the veils of imitation, they too—if they are truthful—should follow his example.

If there is to be any imitation of ancestors, why should they imitate idolaters? They should follow Abraham (peace be upon him).

Secondly, the idolaters against whom Abraham (peace be upon him) rose up relied on this same hollow argument (following their fathers), and Abraham (peace be upon him) never accepted it from them. As the Quran states in Surah Al-Anbiya, verses 53 and 54: "They said: 'We found our fathers worshipping them.' He said: 'Indeed, you and your fathers have been in manifest error!'"

Thirdly, this serves as a form of consolation for the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) and the early Muslims, letting them know that such opposition and excuse-making have always existed; they must not become weak or despondent.

First, He says: "And [remember] when Abraham said to his father (his uncle Azar) and his people: 'Indeed, I am disassociated from that which you worship!'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَ قَوْمِهِ إِنَّنِي بَراءٌ مِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ) [^1]

And since many of the idolaters also worshipped Allah, Abraham immediately excluded Him, saying: "Except Him who created me, for indeed, He will guide me" (إِلَّا الَّذِي فَطَرَنِي فَإِنَّهُ سَيَهْدِينِ).

In this short phrase, he provides both an argument for the exclusivity of servitude to the Lord—since the One worthy of worship is the Creator and Disposer, and everyone accepted that Allah is the Creator—and an allusion to the matter of Allah's ontological and legislative guidance, which the Law of Grace necessitates. [^1] A similar meaning appears in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 77 to 82.

Abraham (peace be upon him) was not only a proponent of the principle of Monotheism and a fighter against all forms of idolatry during his lifetime, but he also exerted all his efforts to ensure that the Word of Monotheism would remain and be established in the world forever. As the next verse says: "And he made it a word enduring among his descendants that they might return [to Allah]" (وَ جَعَلَها كَلِمَةً باقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ) [^2]. It is noteworthy that today, all religions on Earth that profess Monotheism draw inspiration from the monotheistic teachings of Abraham (peace be upon him), and the three great Divine Prophets—Moses (peace be upon him), Jesus (peace be upon him), and Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family)—are from his lineage. This is proof of the truth of the Quran's prophecy in this regard.

It is true that before Abraham (peace be upon him), other prophets such as Noah (peace be upon him) fought against polytheism and idolatry and invited the world toward Monotheism, but the one who gave stability to this Word and raised its banner everywhere was Abraham (peace be upon him), the breaker of idols.

He not only strove immensely during his time for the continuity of the path of Monotheism but also sought this very thing from the Divine Presence in his supplications, saying: "And keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols" (Abraham: 35).

There is another interpretation here: that the pronoun in "made" (جعل) refers to Allah, and the meaning of the sentence becomes: Allah made the Word of Monotheism enduring and established in the lineage of Abraham.

However, the return of the pronoun to Abraham (peace be upon him) himself (the first interpretation) seems more appropriate, as the preceding sentences speak of Abraham and his actions, and it is fitting that this also be part of Abraham's deeds. This is especially so because several verses of the Quran emphasize that Abraham insisted his children and descendants remain upon the Divine religion, as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verses 131 and 132: "When his Lord said to him, 'Submit,' he said, 'I have submitted to the Lord of the worlds.' And Abraham enjoined this upon his sons and [so did] Jacob, [saying], 'O my sons, indeed Allah has chosen for you this religion, so do not die except while you are Muslims.'"

And if it is imagined that the expression "made" (جعل) means creation and is exclusive to Allah, it is an incorrect notion, because "making" (جعل) is also applied to the actions of humans and others, and there are many examples in the Quran. For instance, regarding the brothers throwing Joseph into the well, the Quran uses the term "making" (placing): "So when they took him away and agreed to put (يَجْعَلُوهُ) him into the bottom of the well" (Yusuf: 15). From what we have said, it becomes clear that the object pronoun in "made it" (جعلها) refers to the Word of Monotheism and the testimony of La ilaha illa Allah, which is derived from the phrase "Indeed, I am disassociated from that which you worship." It informs of Abraham's efforts for the continuity of the path of Monotheism in future generations.

In several narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), the referent of the pronoun is identified as the matter of Imamate, and naturally, the subject pronoun then refers to Allah—meaning Allah continued the matter of Imamate in the lineage of Abraham. This is similar to what is understood from verse 124 of Surah Al-Baqarah: when Allah said to Abraham, "I will make you an Imam for the people," he requested that there be Imams among his offspring as well. Allah granted his prayer, except for those who are tainted by wrongdoing: "He said, 'Indeed, I will make you a leader for the people.' [Abraham] said, 'And of my descendants?' [Allah] said, 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers.'"

However, the difficulty at first glance is that there is no mention of Imamate in the verse under discussion, unless it is said that the phrase "He will guide me" (سيهدين) alludes to this meaning, as the guidance of the Prophet and the Imams is a ray of absolute Divine guidance, and the reality of Imamate is one with the reality of guidance.

Better yet, it can be said that the matter of Imamate is included within the Word of Monotheism, because Monotheism has branches, one of which is Monotheism in sovereignty, authority, and leadership. We know that the Imams receive their authority and leadership from Allah, rather than having independence of their own. Thus, these narrations are considered an explanation of an instance and a branch of the general concept of "making it an enduring word."

Therefore, it does not contradict the interpretation mentioned at the beginning (reflect on this). [^1] It is also noteworthy that commentators have given several possibilities for the interpretation of "among his descendants" (في عقبه). Some have interpreted it as all of "Abraham's progeny and lineage" until the end of the world; some have restricted it to Abraham's people and his Ummah; and some have interpreted it as the Family of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family). However, it appears that the concept is broad and encompasses his entire lineage until the end of the world, and the interpretation as the Family of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) is an explanation of a clear instance.

The next verse is, in fact, an answer to several questions, namely: despite this, why does Allah not punish the polytheists of Mecca? Did we not read in the previous verses: "So We took retribution from them" (meaning We took retribution from past nations who denied the prophets and persisted in their ways)?

In response, He says: "Nay, I gave these [polytheists of Mecca] and their fathers enjoyment [of the world] until the truth and a clear Messenger came to them" (بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هؤُلاءِ وَ آباءَهُمْ حَتَّى جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ وَ رَسُولٌ مُبِينٌ).

We did not suffice only with the judgment of reason regarding the falsehood of polytheism and idolatry and the judgment of their conscience regarding Monotheism; rather, We gave them respite to complete the proof until this Heavenly Book, which is entirely Truth, and this great Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) rose for their guidance.

In other words, the phrase "that they might return" in the previous verse shows that the goal of Abraham's (peace be upon him) efforts was for all his lineage to return to the path of Monotheism. While the Arabs claimed to be from the lineage of Abraham, they had not yet returned.

[^1]: According to this interpretation, the exception in the phrase "Except Him who created me" is connected (muttasil), because many idolaters did not deny Allah but rather associated others with Him. However, the possibility has also been raised that the exception is disconnected (munqati'), and "except" (illa) means "but," because the expression "that which you worship" (ما تعبدون) refers to idols, as this expression is not common regarding Allah (reflect carefully).

[^2]: "Aqib" (عقب) originally means the heel of the foot, but it was later used extensively to refer to children and grandchildren.

However, Allah still gave them respite until a great Prophet and a new Book arrived to wake them from this heavy sleep, and a large group did wake up.

But the wonder is that: "When the truth (the Quran) came to them, instead of reforming and making up for their past mistakes, a large group rose in opposition and said: 'This is magic, and indeed we are disbelievers in it'" (وَ لَمَّا جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ قالُوا هذا سِحْرٌ وَ إِنَّا بِهِ كافِرُونَ).

Yes, they called the Quran magic and the great Prophet of Allah a magician; and had they not returned, Divine punishment would have overtaken them.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ (٣١)
And they said, ‘Why was not this Quran sent down to some great man from the two cities?’ (31)
أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ ۚ نَحْنُ قَسَمْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّعِيشَتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَـٰتٍ لِّيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا سُخْرِيًّا ۗ وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (٣٢)
Is it they who dispense the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have dispensed among them their livelihood in the present life, and raised some of them above others in rank, so that some may take others into service, and your Lord’s mercy is better than what they amass. (32)

Commentary

Why was the Quran not revealed to one of the wealthy?

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the pretexts of the polytheists against the call of the Prophets; sometimes they called it sorcery, and at other times they resorted to imitating their ancestors and turned their backs on the Word of God. In the verses under discussion, another of their vain and baseless excuses is mentioned: "And they said, 'Why was this Quran not sent down to a great man from the two cities (Mecca and Ta'if)—a man of wealth and prominence?'" (وَ قالُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلى‌ رَجُلٍ مِنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ‌).

From one perspective, they felt justified in seeking such excuses because, in their view, the criterion for human value was wealth, property, outward status, and fame. These shallow-minded people imagined that the wealthy and the oppressive tribal chiefs were the closest people to Allah. Therefore, they wondered why this gift of prophethood and great divine mercy had not been bestowed upon one of their kind. Conversely, they found it unbelievable that it was revealed to an orphan and a poor, empty-handed man named Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله. Indeed, such a flawed value system led to such an inference. The great calamity of human societies and the primary cause of their intellectual deviation is precisely these false value systems that sometimes portray realities in a completely inverted manner.

The bearer of this divine call must be someone whose entire being is filled with the spirit of piety—a person who is aware, strong-willed, determined, courageous, just, and acquainted with the pain of the deprived and the oppressed. These are the values necessary for carrying this heavenly mission, not beautiful clothes, expensive and magnificent palaces, or various ornaments and luxuries. Notably, none of God's Prophets possessed such [materialistic] conditions, lest authentic values be confused with false ones.

Regarding which specific person in Mecca and Ta'if the excuse-seekers had in mind, there is discussion among commentators. However, most have identified "Walid ibn Mughirah" from Mecca and "Urwah ibn Mas'ud al-Thaqafi" from Ta'if, though some have also mentioned "Utbah ibn Rabi'ah" from Mecca and "Habib ibn 'Amr al-Thaqafi" from Ta'if.

However, their statement apparently did not revolve around a specific individual; rather, their goal was to refer to any person who was wealthy, prominent, and possessed a large clan and tribe.

The Holy Quran provides crushing responses to demolish this ugly and superstitious way of thinking, fully illustrating the Divine and Islamic perspective. First, it says: "Is it they who distribute the mercy of your Lord?" (أَ هُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ‌).

Do they decide to whom they should grant prophethood and upon whom the Heavenly Book should be revealed, and withhold it from whomever they dislike? They are mistaken; your Lord's mercy is distributed by Him alone, and He knows best who is worthy of this great station. As stated in verse 124 of Surah Al-An'am: اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ‌ "Allah knows best where to place His message."

Furthermore, if there is a difference or variation in the level of living among humans, it is by no means evidence of their difference in spiritual ranks. Rather: "We have distributed among them their livelihood in the life of this world and have raised some of them above others in degrees, so that they may employ one another [in service]" (نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ رَفَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجاتٍ لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا).

They have forgotten that human life is a collective life, and managing this life is impossible except through cooperation and mutual service. If all people were at the same level in terms of living and talent, and at the same grade in terms of social status, the principle of cooperation, serving one another, and every human benefiting from others would be destabilized.

Therefore, this difference should not deceive them, nor should they consider it the criterion for human values.

"But the mercy of your Lord is better than whatever they accumulate" (from wealth, prestige, and status) (وَ رَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ‌).

Indeed, all these positions and riches have no weight or value compared to Divine mercy and proximity to the Lord, not even the weight of a fly's wing.

The expression "your Lord" (ربك), which is repeated twice in this verse, is a subtle reference to the special grace of the Lord regarding the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the draping of the garment of prophethood and finality upon his stature.

Key Point: Answering Two Important Questions

Here, some questions arise that often occur when studying the above verse, and which have also been used by the enemies of Islam as a pretext to attack the Islamic worldview.

First: How has the Quran endorsed the employment and "subjugation" of man by man? Is this not an acceptance of an economic class system (the exploiting class and the exploited class)?

Furthermore, if provisions and livelihoods are distributed by Allah, and the differences are all from His side, then what fruit can our efforts and struggles bear? Does this not mean extinguishing the flames of effort and striving for life?

The answers to these questions become clear with careful attention to the text of the verse:

Those who raise such an objection imagine that the concept of the verse is that a specific group of humans should subjugate another group, and that too in the sense of oppressive exploitation. However, this is not the case. Rather, the meaning is the general employment of people relative to one another. This means that every group has specific capabilities, talents, and readiness to be active in a particular field of life; naturally, their services in that field are placed at the disposal of others, just as the services of others in other fields are placed at their disposal. In short, it is a mutual employment and a two-way service. In other words, the goal is cooperation in the matter of living and nothing else.

It goes without saying that if all humans were identical in terms of intelligence, and mental and physical talent, social systems would never be organized. Just as if the cells of the human body were all similar in structure, delicacy, and resistance, the system of the human body would be disrupted. Where are the very strong cells of the heel bone compared to the delicate cells of the retina of the eye? Each of these two has a mission for which they were created.

A vivid example that can be given for this subject is the mutual employment found in the respiratory system, blood circulation, nutrition, and other systems of the human body, which is a clear manifestation of "لِيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً سُخْرِيًّا" (albeit within the scope of the body's internal activities). Can such subjugation be problematic?!

And if it is said that the phrase "We have raised some of them above others in degrees" is evidence of a lack of social justice, we say this would be the case if "justice" were interpreted as "equality" [in every sense]. However, the reality of justice is that everything in an organization is placed in its proper position. Is the existence of a hierarchy in an army, an administrative organization, or a country evidence of the existence of oppression in that system?

Individuals might use the word "equality" everywhere as a slogan without paying attention to its real meaning, but in practice, order is never possible without differences. However, the existence of these differences must never become an excuse for the exploitation of man by man. Everyone must be free to employ their creative powers, flourish their genius, and benefit from the results of their activities without reduction. In cases of inadequacies, those who have power must strive to eliminate them.

As for the second question—how it is possible to keep the flame of struggle and effort burning despite the fact that sustenance is predetermined—the mistake arises from the assumption that Allah has assigned no role to human effort and striving.

It is true that Allah created talents differently for various activities, and it is true that factors outside human will are effective in the course of one's life. Yet, despite this, He has also made human effort and striving one of the fundamental factors. By stating the principle: "That man has nothing but what he strives for" (An-Najm: 39), He has made it clear that a human's benefit in life is closely related to his endeavor and effort.

In any case, the subtle and precise point here is that humans are not like identical containers made in a factory—uniform in shape, size, and type of utility. If they were, they could not live together for even a single day.

Nor are they like the nuts and bolts of a machine that the creator and engineer has adjusted to continue their work by compulsion. Rather, they possess both freedom of will and responsibility and duty, alongside differences in talents and merits. This is the unique mixture called "man," and criticisms and objections often stem from a lack of understanding of this human nature.

In short, Allah has not granted any human superiority over other humans in all aspects. Rather, the phrase "We have raised some of them above others in degrees" refers to the various advantages that each group has over another. The subjugation and employment of each group relative to another stems precisely from these advantages, and this is the essence of justice, management, and wisdom. [1]

[1] In this regard, we have had a detailed discussion in Volume 3 under verse 32 of Surah An-Nisa (page 363) and another discussion in Volume 6 under verse 165 of Surah Al-An'am (page 69).

Ayahs

وَلَوْلَآ أَن يَكُونَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ (٣٣)
Were it not [for the danger] that mankind would be one community, We would have made for those who defy the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairways by which they ascend, (33)
وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَٰبًا وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِـُٔونَ (٣٤)
and [silver] doors for their houses and [silver] couches on which they recline, (34)
وَزُخْرُفًا ۚ وَإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٥)
and ornaments of gold; yet all that would be nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter is for the Godwary near your Lord. (35)

Commentary

Magnificent Palaces with Silver Roofs! (False Values).
