These verses continue the discussion regarding the Islamic value system and the fact that wealth, riches, and material positions are not criteria for merit.

In the first verse, He says: "And were it not that mankind would become one community [of disbelievers], We would have made for those who disbelieve in the Most Compassionate houses with roofs of silver" (وَلَوْلَا أَنْ يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً لَجَعَلْنَا لِمَنْ يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمَنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِنْ فِضَّةٍ) [^1]—houses consisting of multiple stories—"and [silver] stairways and ladders upon which they mount" (وَ مَعارِجَ عَلَيْها يَظْهَرُونَ‌) [^2]. A group of commentators has stated that "stairways" refers to stairs made of silver, and the word "silver" (فضة) is not repeated for the sake of clarity. However, it seems they did not consider the mere existence of stairs as a reason for the importance of the houses, whereas this is not the case; the very existence of numerous stairs is evidence of the grandeur of the building and its multiple floors.

"Suquf" (سقف - with the vowel pattern of 'shotor') is the plural of "Saqf" (ceiling/roof), though some consider it the plural of "Saqifah," meaning a roofed area, but the first opinion is more well-known.

Then He adds: "And for their houses [We would have made] doors and couches upon which they recline" (وَ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْواباً وَ سُرُراً عَلَيْها يَتَّكِؤُنَ‌). It is possible that this sentence refers to doors and couches made of silver, which was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the roofs and thus omitted here to avoid repetition. It is also possible that—

[^1]: "Li-buyutihim" is a 'badal al-ishtimal' (substitutive of inclusion) for "li-man yakfuru bi-l-Rahman," and the repetition of the 'lam' is for this meaning, or it means "ala" (upon), i.e., "upon their houses," but the first possibility is more correct.

[^2]: "Ma'arij" is the plural of "Mi'raj," meaning a means used by a person to ascend to higher floors.

The existence of numerous doors and couches (considering that "abwaban" and "sururan" are indefinite and used here for emphasis/importance) is itself evidence of the grandeur of those palaces. This is because one never places multiple doors for a humble house; this is specific to palaces and magnificent homes, as is the presence of many couches.

Not sufficing with this, He adds: In addition to all this, "and [We would have made for them] ornaments" (وَ زُخْرُفاً) [^1] so that their material and glamorous life would be complete in every respect: magnificent multi-story palaces with silver roofs, numerous doors and couches, various decorative items, and every kind of engraving and design that is the desire, goal, and deity of the world-worshippers. Then He adds: "But all that is nothing but the enjoyment of the life of this world, and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the righteous" (وَ إِنْ كُلُّ ذلِكَ لَمَّا مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

"Zukhruf" originally means any kind of ornament or luxury accompanied by engravings and designs. Since gold is one of the most important means of ornamentation, it is also called "zukhruf." The reason why vain and hollow speech is called "muzakhraf" is because of the outward glitter and polish given to it.

In short, these material riches and the luxurious means of the world are so—

[^1]: Some consider "zukhrufan" to be conjoined to "suqufan," referring to independent decorative items provided to such individuals. Others consider it conjoined to "min fiddatin," meaning it was originally "min zukhrufin" (of gold), then became accusative due to "naz' al-khafid" (omission of the preposition). In this case, the meaning would be that We would have made the roofs, doors, and couches of their houses partly of gold and partly of silver (take note).

worthless in the presence of the Lord that they ought to be the portion only of worthless individuals such as disbelievers and deniers of the truth. If it were not for the fact that weak-willed and world-seeking people would incline toward faithlessness and disbelief, Allah would have granted these riches only to this detested and rejected group, so that everyone would know that these matters are not the measure of human value and character.

Key Points

1- Islam Shatters False Values

Truly, no expression more eloquent than what is found in the above verses can be found to shatter false values. To transform a society where the axis for measuring a person's character is the number of camels, the amount of dirhams and dinars, and the number of male and female slaves, houses, and luxury items—to the point where they wonder why Muhammad, an orphan and materially poor, was chosen for prophethood—the most fundamental task is to shatter these false value frameworks. Upon their ruins, authentic human values such as piety (taqwa), knowledge, altruism, sacrifice, courage, and forgiveness must be built. Otherwise, all reforms will be superficial, surface-level, and unsustainable.

This is exactly what Islam, the Quran, and the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله performed in the most sublime manner. For this reason, a society that was among the most backward and superstitious of human societies grew and developed in a short time to the point where it stood in the front rank.

Interestingly, in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله completing this program, we read: "If the world weighed as much as a gnat's wing in the sight of Allah, He would not have given a disbeliever even a drink of water from it." [^1] The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, also reached the peak of eloquence on this subject in the "Khutbah al-Qasi'ah," saying:

"Musa bin 'Imran entered upon Pharaoh with his brother, while they were wearing woolen clothes and each held a (shepherd's) staff. They made a condition with him that if he submitted to the command of Allah, his kingdom and rule would remain, and his honor and power would endure. But he said: 'Are you not amazed by these two, who condition the survival of my kingdom and the endurance of my honor upon their desire, while poverty and misery are apparent from their appearance? (If they are telling the truth) then why have bracelets of gold not been given to them?'"

"Pharaoh said this because of his glorification of gold and its accumulation, and his contempt for wearing wool."

"If Allah had willed, at the time of sending His prophets, He would have opened for them the doors of treasures, gold mines, and lush green gardens. If He had willed, He would have dispatched the birds of the sky and the wild animals of the earth with them. But if He had done so, the trial of the people would have vanished, and reward and punishment would have become meaningless!"

And in another part of the same sermon, he says:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 250.

"Do you not see that Allah has tested humans, from the time of Adam until the end of the world, with stones that neither harm nor benefit, neither see nor hear? He made these stones His Sacred House (the Kaaba) and made it a means of stability and standing for the people. He established it in the most rocky of places, the most vegetation-less spots on earth, in the narrowness of valleys, among harsh mountains, dense sands, waterless springs, and isolated, distant settlements where no mount can easily live. Then He commanded Adam and his children to turn toward it and make it the center of their gathering..."

"If Allah had willed to place His Sacred House and the site of the Hajj rituals among gardens, rivers, level lands, lush trees, and inhabited areas with many houses and palaces and connected villages, amidst wheat fields and gardens full of flowers and plants, in beautiful, green, and well-watered orchards, in the middle of a delightful garden with easy and developed roads, He would have had the power. But in that case, the trial and test would have been easier, and the reward and punishment would have been less (and people would have become occupied with deceptive outward values and neglected real divine values)." [^1]

In any case, the foundation of the Islamic revolution is a revolution of values. If Muslims today find themselves in difficult and unpleasant conditions under the pressure of ruthless and bloodthirsty enemies, it is because they have abandoned those authentic values, and once again, the values of Jahiliyyah (ignorance) have flourished among them. The measure of character has become wealth and worldly status, and they have consigned knowledge, piety, and virtue to oblivion. They have drowned in material glitter and become estranged from Islam. As long as this remains, the disbelievers must pay for this great error,

[^1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Sermon 192 (Khutbah al-Qasi'ah).

and until they begin the transformation from the values governing their existence, they will not be subject to Divine grace, for: "Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves" (Ar-Ra'd: 11).

2- Answer to a Question

Upon studying the above verses regarding the severe belittling of outward ornaments and material wealth and status, this question arises: Why then does the Holy Quran say elsewhere:

"Say, 'Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has produced for His servants and the good [lawful] things of provision?' Say, 'They are for those who believe during the life of this world [though others share them] and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.' Thus do We detail the verses for a people who know" (Al-A'raf: 32).

Or elsewhere He says: "O children of Adam, take your adornment at every masjid" (Al-A'raf: 31).

How are these two groups of verses compatible?! In response, one must note that the goal in the verses under discussion is to shatter false values. The goal is that the measure of human character should not be considered their wealth and ornaments, not that material facilities are inherently bad. The important thing is that they should be viewed as a tool, not a sublime and final goal.

Furthermore, these things have value only when they are within a reasonable and appropriate limit, free from any extravagance or waste—not the building of palaces of gold and silver and the gathering of massive ornaments of silver and gold! From here it becomes clear that neither the enjoyment of these material blessings by a group of disbelievers and oppressors is evidence of their character, nor is the deprivation of the believers from them, nor does the use of these matters within a reasonable limit as a tool cause any harm to a person's faith and piety. This is the correct Islamic and Quranic thinking.

Ayahs

وَمَن يَعْشُ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُۥ شَيْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ لَهُۥ قَرِينٌ (٣٦)
We assign a devil to be the companion of him who turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent. (36)
وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٣٧)
Indeed they bar them from the way [of Allah], while they suppose that they are [rightly] guided. (37)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَنَا قَالَ يَـٰلَيْتَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرِينُ (٣٨)
When he comes to Us, he will say, ‘I wish there had been between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion [you are]!’ (38)
وَلَن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ إِذ ظَّلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ (٣٩)
‘Today that will be of no avail to you. As you did wrong, so will you share in the punishment.’ (39)
أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ أَوْ تَهْدِى ٱلْعُمْىَ وَمَن كَانَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٤٠)
Can you, then, make the deaf hear or guide the blind and those who are in manifest error? (40)

Commentary

Companions of the Devils!

Since the preceding verses spoke of the world-worshippers who evaluate everything by material standards, the verses under discussion speak of one of the deadly effects of attachment to the world, which is alienation from God.

It says: "And whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Most Compassionate, We appoint for him a devil, and he will be his constant companion!" (وَ مَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ) [^1] [^2] Indeed, negligence of the remembrance of Allah, drowning in worldly pleasures, and infatuation with its glitz and glamour cause a devil to gain mastery over man and become his constant companion, casting a rope around his neck and "leading him wherever he desires!" It is obvious that there is no room for anyone to imagine predestination (jabr) from this verse, because this is the result of the actions they themselves have performed. We have said many times that human actions—especially drowning in worldly pleasures and becoming tainted with various sins—have their first effect in

[^1]: "Ya'shu" (يعش) is from the root "ashw" (عشو). When it is transitive with "ila" (عشوت اليه), it means to be guided by something with weak eyes; when it is transitive with "an" (عشو عنه), it means to turn away and avert oneself from something, and in the verse under discussion, this is the intended meaning (Lisan al-Arab, root 'ashw).

[^2]: "Nuqayyid" (نقيض) is from the root "qayd" (قيض), which originally means the shell of an egg, and subsequently came to mean making one thing dominate another.

placing a veil over the human heart, eyes, and ears, alienating him from Allah, and allowing devils to dominate him. This continues to the point where sometimes the path of return is closed to him, because devils and satanic thoughts surround him from every side. This is the result of man's own action, although attributing it to Allah is also correct in the sense of Him being the "Cause of causes." This is the same thing expressed in other verses of the Quran as the "adornment of Satan" (فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ) (Nahl-63) or the "guardianship of Satan" (فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ) (Nahl-63).

It is noteworthy that the word "Nuqayyid," considering its literal meaning, indicates both the mastery of the devils and their companionship. At the same time, the phrase "He is a companion to him" (هو له قرين) follows it to emphasize the meaning that the devils never separate from such individuals! The use of the name "Ar-Rahman" (The Most Compassionate) is a subtle allusion: how can they turn away from the God whose general mercy encompasses everyone and become heedless of His remembrance?

Can such people have any fate other than being companions of devils and subject to their command?

Some commentators have suggested that "devils" here has a broad meaning that even includes human devils, considering it an allusion to the "leaders and chiefs of misguidance" who dominate and master those heedless of Allah's remembrance and are their companions; this expansion of meaning is not far-fetched.

Then, it points to two important things that the devils do regarding these heedless ones, saying: "And indeed, they avert them from the [right] way" (وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ) [^1]. Whenever they intend to return, they throw a stone in their path and create an obstacle so that they never return to the Straight Path.

And they so beautify the path of misguidance in their eyes that: "they think they are [rightly] guided!" (وَ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ).

Just as we read in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 38, regarding "Ad" and "Thamud":

وَ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَ كانُوا مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ:

"And Satan made their deeds seem fair to them and averted them from the path, though they were endowed with perception."

In short, this situation continues—the heedless and unaware man in his misguidance, and the devils in misleading him—until the veils are removed and his truth-seeking eye is opened. "When he comes to Us, he sees that his guardian and companion is still with him, the very one who was the cause of all his misfortunes! He cries out and says: 'O, I wish there were between me and you the distance of the two Easts! What a wretched companion you are!'" (حَتَّى إِذا جاءَنا قالَ يا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ).

All punishments on one side, and the companionship of this evil associate on the other! Companionship with a devil such that whenever he looks at his repulsive face, all the memories of his misguidance and misfortune are personified before him; the one who made ugliness seem beautiful to him, the detour seem like the highway, and misguidance seem like guidance. Alas, he is forever

[^1]: The plural pronoun in "innahum" (انهم) and the following phrase refers back to "devils," even though it appeared in the singular form previously, because it actually carried a collective meaning.

his companion and fellow prisoner!

Yes, the scene of Resurrection is a vast manifestation of the scenes of this world, and the companion, friend, and leader here is the same as there. According to some commentators, they are even bound together with a single chain!

It is clear that "the two Easts" (مشرقين) refers to "the East and the West," because according to Arabic custom, when they want to make a dual form of two different types, they choose the word from one of them, such as "Shamsayn" (referring to the sun and the moon), "Zuhrayn" (referring to Dhuhr and Asr prayers), and "Isha'ayn" (referring to Maghrib and Isha prayers).

Other interpretations have also been mentioned which do not seem appropriate at all for the verse under discussion, such as the sunrise of the beginning of winter and the sunrise of the beginning of summer, although they are appropriate in other contexts.

In any case, this expression is a metaphor for the furthest distance imaginable, as "the distance between East and West" is a well-known proverb in this regard.

But this wish never leads anywhere, and there is never a separation between them and the devils. Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "And it will never benefit you today, since you did wrong, that you are sharers in the punishment" (وَ لَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ إِذْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ). You must endure the punishment of this evil companion along with other punishments forever. [^1]

[^1]: Accordingly, the subject of "yanfa'" (benefit) is the previous speech where they wished the distance between them and the devils was the distance between the West and the East, and the phrase "idh dhalamtum" (since you did wrong) explains the reason for the lack of benefit, and the phrase "annakum fil-adhabi mushtarikun" (that you are sharers in the punishment) is the result of this injustice and oppression.

In this way, He turns their hope regarding separation from the devils into despair forever—and how agonizing it is to endure this companionship! In the interpretation of this verse, other possibilities have also been given, including that sometimes a person's pain is alleviated by seeing other sufferers, as it is famously said: "When a calamity becomes general, it becomes bearable!" But it is said to them that there is no such consolation there; you are so drowned in punishment that the punishment of the devil bound to you will not be a source of consolation for you. [^1] The possibility has also been raised that sometimes a misfortune arrives and a person divides its consequences between himself and his friends, and the burden of the misfortune becomes light, but there too, this issue does not exist, because each has a full share of the divine punishment without anything being reduced from the other!

However, considering that this verse is a completion of the previous verse, the first interpretation we chose is more appropriate.

Here, the Quran leaves this group to themselves and turns the address toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, discussing the blind-hearted heedless ones who constantly denied him and were of the same sort as the group mentioned in the previous verses, saying: "Can you then make the deaf hear? Or guide the blind and those who are in manifest error and do not perceive?" (أَ فَأَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ أَوْ تَهْدِي الْعُمْيَ وَ مَنْ كانَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ). A similar meaning has appeared in other verses of the Holy Quran, where

[^1]: According to this interpretation, the phrase "annakum fil-adhabi mushtarikun" would be the subject of "yanfa'," not its result.

stubborn and unguidatable individuals, and those who are sightless followers of whims and drowned in sin, are compared to the deaf and the blind, and sometimes to the dead.

In Surah Yunus, verse 42, we read: أَ فَأَنْتَ تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يَعْقِلُونَ:

"Can you then make the deaf hear, even though they do not use reason?" And in Surah An-Naml, verse 80, it says: إِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى‌ وَ لا تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ الدُّعاءَ إِذا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ: "Indeed, you cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs and flee," as well as other verses.

These expressions are all because the Quran recognizes "two types of ears, eyes, and life" for man: outward ears, eyes, and life, and inward ears, eyes, and life.

The important part is the second type of perception, vision, and life, which when it ceases to function, neither advice nor counsel will be useful, nor will warnings and alarms! It is noteworthy that: in the previous verses, this group of people was compared to individuals who have weak eyes and limited vision, and in the latest verse, it compares them to the deaf and the blind. This is because when a person becomes occupied with the world, he is like someone whose eyes have developed a slight pain; the more his preoccupation with the world, the more intense his inclination toward material things, and the greater his indifference toward spiritual matters, that eye pain leads to a decrease in vision, and from there, it reaches the stage of blindness. This is the same thing that definitive evidence has proven regarding the intensification of negative and positive spiritual states in man and the rooting of traits in his being due to repetition and persistence in action, and the Quran has also observed this order. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi, Vol. 27, pp. 214 and 215.

Ayahs

فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُم مُّنتَقِمُونَ (٤١)
We will indeed take vengeance on them, whether We take you away (41)
أَوْ نُرِيَنَّكَ ٱلَّذِى وَعَدْنَـٰهُمْ فَإِنَّا عَلَيْهِم مُّقْتَدِرُونَ (٤٢)
or show you what We have promised them, for indeed We hold them in Our power. (42)
فَٱسْتَمْسِكْ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٤٣)
So hold fast to what has been revealed to you. Indeed, you are on a straight path. (43)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذِكْرٌ لَّكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ تُسْـَٔلُونَ (٤٤)
Indeed it is a reminder for you and your people, and soon you will be questioned. (44)
وَسْـَٔلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَآ أَجَعَلْنَا مِن دُونِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ءَالِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ (٤٥)
Ask those of Our apostles We have sent before you: Did We set up any gods to be worshipped besides the All-beneficent? (45)

Commentary

Hold Fast to What Has Been Revealed to You

Following the previous verses which spoke of the stubborn, unguided, and oppressive disbelievers, the verses under discussion address the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), severely threatening this group while granting the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) solace and peace of mind. It states: "If We take you away from among them, We shall surely take vengeance on them and punish them!" (فَإِنَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُمْ مُنْتَقِمُونَ).

The meaning of "taking the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) away" from that people could refer to either the Prophet's passing or his migration from Mecca to Medina. In either case, it indicates that even if you are not a witness and observer, and they continue in their ways, We will severely punish them. This is because "vengeance" (intiqam) originally means punishment and retribution. However, it can be inferred from several other verses revealed in the Quran with the same meaning that "taking the Prophet away" refers to his passing, as stated in Surah Yunus, verse 46: وَ إِمَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلى‌ ما يَفْعَلُونَ ("Whether We show you some of what We promise them or We take you away [in death] and you do not see them, in any case, their return is to Us, and Allah is Witness over what they do").

The same meaning appears in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 40, and Surah Ghafir, verse 77; thus, interpreting the verse as referring to the migration (Hijrah) does not seem appropriate.

Then it adds: "Or if We show you that which We have promised them, We indeed have full power over them" (أَوْ نُرِيَنَّكَ الَّذِي وَعَدْناهُمْ فَإِنَّا عَلَيْهِمْ مُقْتَدِرُونَ). In any case, they are within the grasp of Our power, whether you are among them or not, and divine punishment and vengeance are certain if they continue their actions; it may be sooner or later, but it will never fail to occur, whether during your lifetime or after your passing.

These Quranic emphases might, on one hand, refer to the impatience of the disbelievers who would say: "If you are truthful, then why does the torment not descend upon us?!" And on the other hand, they were awaiting the Prophet's death, imagining that once he was no longer present, everything would come to an end.

After these warnings, He commands the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "So hold fast to that which has been revealed to you; indeed, you are on a straight path!" (فَاسْتَمْسِكْ بِالَّذِي أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ عَلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ). There is not the slightest deviation or crookedness in your Book and program, and the rejection by a group of them will not be a reason to deny your truthfulness. Continue your path with utmost seriousness; the rest is up to Us.

Then He adds: "And indeed, this [Quran] which has been revealed to you is a reminder for you and your people" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَ لِقَوْمِكَ). The purpose of its revelation is to awaken human beings and acquaint them with their duties.

"And soon you will be questioned" about what you did with this divine program and this heavenly revelation! (وَ سَوْفَ تُسْئَلُونَ).

According to this commentary, "reminder" (dhikr) in the above verse means "remembrance of Allah" and awareness of religious obligations, just as it appears with this meaning in verses 5 and 36 of this Surah, as well as many other verses of the Quran.

Fundamentally, one of the names of the Holy Quran is "Ad-Dhikr," meaning the Reminder and the Remembrance of Allah. We repeatedly read this sentence in Surah Al-Qamar: وَ لَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ ("And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?") (Surah Al-Qamar, verses 17, 22, 32, and 40).

Furthermore, the phrase "and soon you will be questioned" testifies that the intent is questioning regarding the implementation of this divine program.

However, despite all this, it is strange that many commentators have chosen another interpretation for this verse which does not align with what we have said. Among other things, they have said the meaning of the verse is: "This Quran is a source of honor and reputation or a good mention for you and your people, and it grants nobility to the Arabs and the Quraysh or your Ummah, because it was revealed in their language, and soon they will be questioned about this divine blessing." [1] It is true that the Holy Quran raised the renown of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the Arabs, and indeed all Muslims in the world. For more than fourteen centuries, the name of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) has been proclaimed with greatness every morning and evening from the minarets, and the nameless and signless people of the pre-Islamic era (Jahiliyyah) found a name and identity under its shadow, and the Islamic Ummah became world-renowned in its light.

It is also true that "dhikr" has sometimes appeared with this meaning in the Holy Quran, but undoubtedly the first meaning is more extensive in Quranic verses and more consistent with the purpose of the Quran's revelation and the verses under discussion.

Some commentators have taken verse 10 of Surah Al-Anbiya as evidence for the second interpretation: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ كِتاباً فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ ("We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your reminder; will you not then reason?") [2]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr Razi, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir al-Maraghi, and Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi under the verses discussed. [2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the verses discussed.

While that verse is also consistent with the first interpretation, as we explained in volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh under that same verse. [1] Narrations have been recorded regarding this verse in hadith sources which will be mentioned in the "Key Points" section.

Then, to negate idol worship and invalidate the beliefs of the polytheists, He turns to another proof, saying: "Ask those of Our messengers whom We sent before you: did We ever appoint gods to be worshipped besides the Most Merciful?" (وَ سْئَلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا أَ جَعَلْنا مِنْ دُونِ الرَّحْمنِ آلِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ). This is an indication that all divine prophets called toward monotheism (Tawhid), and all of them categorically condemned idol worship. Therefore, the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) has not done something unprecedented in his opposition to idols, but rather has revived the perpetual tradition of the prophets. It is these idolaters and polytheists who are stepping contrary to the school of all the prophets.

According to this interpretation, although the questioner is the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the intended audience is the entire Ummah and even his opponents.

And those who are questioned are the followers of the previous prophets—their truthful and reliable followers, and even their ordinary individuals—because from the collective body of their words, "successive reports" (khabar mutawatir) are obtained which express the monotheistic school of the prophets.

It is necessary to mention that even those who deviated from the principle of monotheism (like the Christians of today who advocate for the Trinity) still claim monotheism and say that our Trinity does not contradict monotheism, which is the religion of all prophets! Thus, referring to these nations is sufficient to invalidate the claim of the polytheists.

However, a group of commentators have given another possibility in interpreting the verse, inspired by some narrations [1], which is that the questioner is the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and those questioned are the previous prophets themselves. They then added that this matter took place on the Night of Ascension (Mi'raj), as the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came into contact with the souls of the previous prophets, and to emphasize the matter of monotheism, he raised this question and heard the answer.

Some have also added that this connection was possible for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) even outside the Night of Ascension, as temporal and spatial distances would never create an obstacle in the connection of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) with the souls of the prophets, and that great man could connect with them at any moment and in any place.

Of course, these interpretations present no intellectual problem, but since the goal of the verse is to negate the religion of the polytheists rather than to bring peace to the Prophet's soul—for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was so immersed in the matter of monotheism and so averse to polytheism that he had no need to ask—and for the sake of arguing against the polytheists, the Prophet's spiritual contact with the souls of previous prophets would not be convincing [to them]. Therefore, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, and the second interpretation may refer to the inner meanings (batn) of the verse rather than its outward appearance (zahir), as the verses of the Quran have both an outward and an inward dimension.

It is also noteworthy that among the names of Allah in the above verse, emphasis is placed on the name "Ar-Rahman" (the Most Merciful), indicating how it is possible to abandon the God whose general mercy encompasses everyone and go toward idols that are the source of neither benefit nor harm.

Key Point: Who are the Prophet's People?

Regarding who is meant by "people" (qawm) in the verse "وَ إِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَ لِقَوْمِكَ," three possibilities exist: First, the entirety of the Islamic Ummah; second, the Arab people; and third, the tribe of Quraysh.

Since "people" in the logic of the Quran is applied in many cases to the nations of the prophets or their contemporary peoples, it seems that in the above verse, this same meaning is intended.

Therefore, the Quran is a source of reminder and awareness for the entire Islamic Ummah (according to the first interpretation) and a source of pride and honor for all of them (according to the second interpretation).

However, in several narrations reached from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام), we read that the Infallible Imams would say that the meaning of "people" in this verse is "us, the family of the Prophet." [1] But it is not unlikely that these are among the clear examples (masadiq), whether the concept of "people" was the entire Islamic Ummah, or the Arab people, or the tribe of the Prophet of Islam; in any case, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام) are considered among its clearest examples.

[1]: Another matter that might be evidence for the famous interpretation is the word "people" (qawm) mentioned in the verse, because the Quran is a source of reminder for all the worlds, not just the Prophet's people or the Islamic nation. However, this point can also be answered, as this group benefited from the reminders of the Quran before others, and for this reason, they were emphasized.

[1]: These hadiths have been collected by the author of Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn in volume 4, pages 64 and 65.

[1]: This narration is quoted from Ibn Abbas in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr Razi, and Majma' al-Bayan. In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, two detailed narrations in this regard are quoted from the book Al-Ihtijaj and Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim (refer to volume 4, pages 605 to 607).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَقَالَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٦)
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs to Pharaoh and his elite. He said, ‘I am indeed an apostle of the Lord of all the worlds.’ (46)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ (٤٧)
But when he brought them Our signs, they indeed laughed at them. (47)
وَمَا نُرِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ إِلَّا هِىَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا ۖ وَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (٤٨)
We did not show them a sign but it was greater than the other, and We visited on them punishment so that they might come back. (48)
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَ ٱلسَّاحِرُ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ (٤٩)
They would say, ‘O magician! Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you [to remove this scourge]. We will indeed be guided [when it is removed].’ (49)
فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ (٥٠)
But when We lifted the punishment from them, behold, they would break their pledge. (50)

Commentary

The Arrogant and Covenant-Breaking Pharaohs

In these verses, a portion of the story of the Prophet of God, "Musa bin Imran" (peace be upon him), and his encounter with "Pharaoh" is mentioned. This serves as a response to the baseless claims of the polytheists who asked: "If Allah wanted to send a messenger, why did He not choose a man from the wealthy of Mecca and Ta'if for this great mission?"

This is because "Pharaoh" held the same objection against Musa (peace be upon him), and his logic was exactly the same; Pharaoh reproached and rebuked him for his woolen clothes and his lack of gold ornaments.

In the first verse, He says: "And We certainly sent Musa with Our signs to Pharaoh and his establishment and courtiers" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِآيَاتِنَا إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَئِهِ).

"Musa said to them: 'Indeed, I am the messenger of the Lord of the worlds'" (فَقَالَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ).

The meaning of "signs" (آيات) refers to the miracles Musa (peace be upon him) possessed, through which he proved his truthfulness, the most important of which were the miracle of the "Staff" and the "Radiant Hand."

And "Mala'" (ملاء), as we have mentioned before, is derived from the root "Mala" (to fill) and refers to a group that pursues a common goal and whose appearance "fills the eye" (impressive/prominent). In the Holy Quran, this word is usually applied to the nobles, the wealthy, or the courtiers.

The emphasis on the title "Lord of the worlds" (رب العالمين) is, in fact, a statement of the claim accompanied by its proof; for only He who is the Lord of the worlds, their Owner, and their Nurturer is worthy of worship, not needy and dependent creatures like the Pharaohs and idols!

Now let us see what the first reaction of Pharaoh and his people was to the logical proofs and clear miracles of Musa (peace be upon him). The Quran says in the next verse: "But when he came to them with Our signs, at once they laughed at them" (فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ بِآيَاتِنَا إِذَا هُمْ مِنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ).

This is the first reaction of all tyrants and arrogant ignorant ones toward true leaders: not taking their invitation and proofs seriously, and responding to everything with mockery and ridicule, so as to make others understand that their invitation is not even worth investigating, studying, or answering, and does not merit a serious encounter.

However, to complete the proof, We sent Our signs one after another, "And We showed them no sign but that it was greater than its predecessor" (وَمَا نُرِيهِمْ مِنْ آيَةٍ إِلَّا هِيَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا) [1]. In short, We presented Our signs to them, each more important, more eloquent, and more crushing than the other, so that no excuse remained for them, and they might descend from the steed of pride, vanity, and selfishness.

Thus, after the miracle of the "Staff" and the "Radiant Hand," We showed them the miracles of the "Flood," "Locusts," "Lice," "Frogs," and others [2].

[1]: The expression "Ukht" (sister) in the Arabic language refers to something of the same rank or kind, like two sisters in relation to one another.

[2]: The explanation of the nine miracles of Musa bin Imran (peace be upon him) is provided under verse 101 of Surah Al-Isra (Volume 12, page 309).

Then He adds: We seized them with warning afflictions and punishments "that perhaps they might return" to the path of truth (وَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ بِالْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ).

Drought, famine, and shortage of fruits came upon them, as stated in verse 130 of Surah Al-A'raf: "And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and deficiency in fruits."

Sometimes the color of the Nile water turned to the "color of blood," making it unusable for drinking or agriculture, and sometimes plant pests destroyed their crops.

These bitter and painful events, although they temporarily awakened them and caused them to cling to Musa (peace be upon him), as soon as the storm of the incident subsided, they would forget everything and target Musa (peace be upon him) with various arrows of slander.

As we read in the next verse: "And they said, 'O magician! Invoke for us your Lord by what He has promised you [that He will remove the plague], for indeed we will be guided!'" (وَقَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا السَّاحِرُ ادْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِنْدَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ).

What a strange expression! On one hand, they call him a magician; on the other hand, they cling to him to remove the affliction; and on a third hand, they promise him to accept guidance! The apparent inconsistency between these three matters has led to various interpretations:

Some have said "Magician" (ساحر) here means "Scholar" (عالم), because at that time, and especially in the environment of Egypt, magicians were respected and viewed as learned men.

Some have suggested that magic here means performing a great task, just as we say in everyday expressions: "So-and-so is so skilled in his work, it is as if he performs magic!"

And sometimes it is said that "magician" refers to how he was perceived in the minds of a group of people.

And other such interpretations.

However, those who are familiar with the mindset and speech of self-satisfied ignorant ones, arrogant tyrants, and despotic oppressors know that they have many such contradictory expressions. It is not surprising that they call him a magician at the beginning, then cling to him, and finally promise to accept guidance.

Therefore, the literal meaning of the verse's expressions should be maintained, and there seems to be no need for other justifications or interpretations.

In any case, it was clear from their tone that while feeling a need for Musa (peace be upon him), they were giving him false promises. Even in their state of helplessness and when presenting their need, they did not descend from the steed of pride. For this reason, they used the expressions "your Lord" (ربك) and "by what He has promised you" (بما عهد عندك), and never said "our Lord" or "the promise He made to us," even though Musa (peace be upon him) had explicitly told them, "I am the messenger of the Lord of the worlds," not just my own Lord.

Yes, such is the logic and way of the arrogant light-headed ones when they sit upon the throne of power.

Nevertheless, Musa (peace be upon him) never gave up on guiding them because of these stinging and insulting expressions, nor did he become despondent or tired of their stubbornness. He continued his work, praying many times so that the storm of afflictions would subside, and it did. But as the next verse says: "But when We removed the punishment from them, at once they broke their word" (فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ).

These are all living and eloquent lessons for Muslims and a consolation for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) himself, so that they never grow weary of the stubbornness and persistence of the opponents, and so that the dust of despair and hopelessness does not settle on their souls. They should know that "this sweet water and salty water flow in veins" (good and evil coexist in history), and they should continue their path with even more steadfastness and perseverance, just as Musa (peace be upon him) and the Children of Israel continued and finally triumphed over the Pharaohs.

It is also a warning to stubborn and persistent enemies that they are never stronger or more powerful than Pharaoh and his people; they should see the end of their affair and reflect upon their own destiny.

Ayahs

وَنَادَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى قَوْمِهِۦ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِى مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِىٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٥١)
And Pharaoh proclaimed to his people, saying, ‘O my people! Do not the kingdom of Egypt and these rivers that run at my feet belong to me? Do you not perceive? (51)
أَمْ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ (٥٢)
Am I not better than this wretch who cannot even speak clearly? (52)
فَلَوْلَآ أُلْقِىَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِّن ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ (٥٣)
Why no bracelets of gold have been thrown to him, nor any angels accompany him as escorts?’ (53)
فَٱسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُۥ فَأَطَاعُوهُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٥٤)
Thus did he mislead his people and they obeyed him. Indeed, they were a transgressing lot. (54)
فَلَمَّآ ءَاسَفُونَا ٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥٥)
So when they roused Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all. (55)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ سَلَفًا وَمَثَلًا لِّلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٥٦)
Thus We made them the vanguard and an example for posterity. (56)

Commentary

If He is a Prophet, Why Does He Not Have Gold Bracelets?!

The logic of Moses (peace be upon him) on one hand, his various miracles on the other, and the plagues that befell the people of Egypt—which were lifted through the blessing of Moses' (peace be upon him) prayers—on the third hand, left a profound impact on the environment. It shook the thoughts of the masses regarding Pharaoh and called their entire religious and social system into question.

It was at this point that Pharaoh, through sophistry and fallacy, sought to prevent the influence of Moses (peace be upon him) on the minds of the Egyptian people. He resorted to the base values that dominated that environment and compared himself to Moses using these values to prove his own superiority, as the Quran states in the verses under discussion:

"Pharaoh proclaimed among his people, saying: 'O my people! Does not the dominion of the vast land of Egypt belong to me? And do these great rivers not flow under my command? Do they not pass by my palace, farms, and gardens? Do you not see?'" (وَ نادى‌ فِرْعَوْنُ فِي قَوْمِهِ قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَ لَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَ هذِهِ الْأَنْهارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي أَ فَلا تُبْصِرُونَ‌) [^1] But what does Moses have? Nothing—a staff and a woolen garment! Does the prestige belong to him or to me? Does he speak the truth, or do I? Open your eyes and look at the matter correctly.

In this way, Pharaoh paraded fake values before the eyes of the Egyptian people, and just like the idolaters of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) in the face of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), he took wealth and status as the true human values.

The expression "proclaimed" (نادى) indicates that Pharaoh organized a great assembly of the country's elites and addressed them all with a loud, resonant voice, recounting these sentences, or he ordered that his proclamation be echoed throughout the country as an official decree.

The use of "rivers" (انهار), the plural of "river" (نهر), even though the Nile River is intended, is because this great river—which is like a vast sea—was divided into many branches and irrigated all the inhabited regions of Egypt.

Some commentators have said the Nile River had 360 branches, the most important of which were the "King's River," "Tulun River," "Damietta River," and "Tinnis River."

Why does Pharaoh specifically emphasize the branches of the Nile? Because all of Egypt's prosperity, wealth, power, and civilization originated from the Nile; therefore, Pharaoh boasts about it and prides himself over Moses! The expression "flow under me" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي‌) does not mean that the Nile flows beneath my palace, as a group of commentators have suggested—for the Nile was too grand to flow under Pharaoh's palace. If the meaning were "beside his palace," many Egyptian palaces were like that, and most settlements were located on the two banks of this great river. Rather, it means: "This river is under my command, and the system of its distribution to the settlements follows the regulations that I decree."

Then he adds: "Undoubtedly, I am better than this individual who is of low status and race, and can hardly speak clearly" (أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَ لا يَكادُ يُبِينُ‌) [^1]. In this way, he stated two great points of pride for himself (the rule of Egypt and ownership of the Nile) and two points of weakness for Moses (poverty and a speech impediment).

This is while Moses (peace be upon him) certainly did not have a speech impediment at that time, for Allah had answered his prayer and removed the heaviness from his tongue. At the time of his appointment (Bi'thah), he had pleaded: "And untie the knot from my tongue" (وَ احْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي‌) (Ta-Ha: 27), and his prayer was certainly answered, as the Quran also testifies.

Not possessing vast wealth, magnificent clothes, and glittering palaces—which are usually obtained through the oppression of the deprived—is not only not a flaw but is a source of pride, dignity, and value.

The term "despicable/lowly" (مهين) might refer to the social classes of that time, where the wealthy and the powerful aristocrats were considered the upper class, and the low-income laborers were considered the lower class. Or it might refer to Moses' race, as he was from the Children of Israel, while the Pharaonic Copts considered themselves their masters and lords.

Then Pharaoh clung to two other excuses, saying: "Why have bracelets of gold not been given to him?! Or why have angels not come with him to confirm his words?!" (فَلَوْ لا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جاءَ مَعَهُ الْمَلائِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ‌) ^2

[^1]: The "Waw" in the sentence "وَ هذِهِ الْأَنْهارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي‌" may be conjunctive (linked to "the dominion of Egypt") or circumstantial (Tafsir al-Kashshaf), but the first possibility seems more appropriate. [^1]: A group has taken "Am" (أَمْ) in the above sentence as "disjunctive" (Munqati'ah) meaning "Nay" (Bal), while others consider it "conjunctive" (Muttasilah) related to the sentence "Do you not see?" (أَ فَلا تُبْصِرُونَ‌), implying: "Do you not see, or do you see that I am better than this...?"

If Allah has made him His messenger, why has He not given him gold bracelets like other messengers and provided him with helpers and assistants?

It is said that the Pharaonians believed that leaders must adorn themselves with gold bracelets and necklaces; therefore, he expressed surprise that Moses did not have such ornaments and instead wore the woolen clothes of a shepherd. Such is the state of a people whose criterion for measuring personality is gold, silver, and ornaments.

However, the Divine Prophets specifically wanted to invalidate these false and fake values by distancing themselves from such matters, and to replace them with authentic human values—namely knowledge, piety, and purity—because until the value system of a society is reformed, that society will never see prosperity.

In any case, this excuse of Pharaoh was exactly like the excuse mentioned a few verses earlier from the polytheists of Mecca, who said: "Why was this Quran not revealed to one of the wealthy men of Mecca or Ta'if?!" The second excuse is the well-known one that many misguided and rebellious nations raised against the prophets: sometimes they said, "Why is he a human and not an angel?" and sometimes they said, "If he is a human, why at least has an angel not come with him?"

In reality, messengers sent to humans must be of their own kind so they can feel their needs, problems, and issues, respond to them, and be able to serve as a practical model and example for them. [^1] It is worth noting that "Aswirah" (أَسْوِرَةٌ) is the plural of "Siwar" (سوار), meaning bracelet, whether of gold or silver, and its origin is taken from the Persian word "Dastvarah" (اساور is also a plural of the plural).

In the next verse, the Quran points to a subtle point: Pharaoh was not entirely unaware of the reality of the matter and was more or less aware of the worthlessness of these values, but "he fooled his people, and they obeyed him, for they were a light-headed people" (فَاسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُ فَأَطاعُوهُ‌). Fundamentally, the way of all tyrannical and corrupt governments is that to continue their autocracy, they must keep the people at a low level of thought and reflection, and fool them with various means. They plunge them into a state of ignorance regarding realities and replace true values with false ones, constantly brainwashing them against the truth.

This is because the awakening of nations and their awareness and intellectual growth is the greatest enemy of autocratic and satanic governments, which they fight with all their might! This Pharaonic method—the belittling of intellects—is prevalent with full intensity in our era across all corrupt societies. If Pharaoh had limited means to achieve this goal, today's tyrants (Taghuts) use mass media, the press, radio and television broadcasts, various films, and even sports in a deviant form, and the invention of various ridiculous fashions to belittle the intellects of nations, so they fall into complete ignorance and obey them. For this reason, scholars and religious devotees who continue the intellectual and school-of-thought line of the prophets have a heavy responsibility in fighting the program of "belittling intellects," which is among their most important duties.

Interestingly, the verse concludes and ends with this sentence: "They were a group of transgressors" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ‌). This indicates that if these misguided people were not transgressors and had not stepped outside the command of Allah and the judgment of reason, they would not have submitted to such propaganda and nonsense, and would not have provided the means for their own misguidance. For this reason, they were never excused in this misguidance. It is true that Pharaoh stole their reason and forced them into his obedience, but they also provided the grounds for this theft through "blind submission." Yes, they were transgressors who followed a transgressor.

These were the crimes of Pharaoh and his people and their fallacies against the Divine messenger Moses (peace be upon him). But now let us see, after all that preaching, guidance, and the completion of the proof through various ways, and their refusal to submit to the truth, where did their end lead?! He says: "When they provoked Our wrath with their deeds, We took retribution from them and drowned them all!" (فَلَمَّا آسَفُونا انْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

Allah specifically chose the punishment of drowning for them from among all punishments, because all their glory, pomp, pride, and power were tied to that same great Nile River and its large and numerous branches, upon which Pharaoh relied among all his sources of power, saying: "Does not the dominion of Egypt belong to me, and these rivers flow under my command?" Yes, the very thing that was the source of their life and power must become the cause of their annihilation, destruction, and their graveyard, so that all may take heed!

[^1]: In the fifth volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verse 9 of Surah Al-An'am, there is a detailed discussion on this subject (page 159).

"Asafuna" (آسَفُونا) comes from the root "Asaf" (اسف), which means both "grief" and "wrath." Indeed, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it is sometimes used for "grief coupled with wrath," and sometimes for each of these two separately, because its reality is an internal excitement that calls a person to retribution. Whenever it is toward subordinates, it appears in the form of wrath; whenever it is toward superiors, it appears in the form of grief. Thus, when Ibn Abbas was asked about "sorrow" and "wrath," he said the root of both is one, but the wording is different! [^1] Some commentators have interpreted "Asafuna" as "they grieved Our messengers" (آسَفُوا رُسُلَنا), but this interpretation seems unlikely, and there is no necessity for such a departure from the literal meaning.

It is also noteworthy that neither grief nor sorrow has any meaning regarding Allah, nor does wrath in the sense commonly known among us. Rather, the wrath and anger of Allah mean the "will to punish," and His pleasure means the "will to reward."

In the final verse under discussion, as a conclusion to this entire discourse, He says: "We made them a precedent in punishment and an example for others" (فَجَعَلْناهُمْ سَلَفاً وَ مَثَلًا لِلْآخِرِينَ‌). "Salaf" (سَلَف) linguistically means anything that precedes; hence, previous generations are called "Salaf" and the generations after them are called "Khalaf." Transactions conducted as pre-purchases are also called "Salaf" because the price is paid beforehand.

[^1]: Al-Mufradat by Raghib, under the root "Asaf."

And "Mathal" (مَثَل) refers to a saying that becomes common and current among people as a lesson. Since the story of the life of Pharaoh and his people and their painful fate was a great lesson, it is mentioned in this verse as a "Mathal" (example/parable) for other nations.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَمَّا ضُرِبَ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا إِذَا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ (٥٧)
When the Son of Mary was cited as an example, behold, your people raise an outcry. (57)

They say, ‘Are our gods better or he?’ They cite him to you only for the sake of contention. Indeed, they are a contentious lot. (58)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَثَلًا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٥٩)
He was just a servant whom We had blessed and made an exemplar for the Children of Israel. (59)
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَجَعَلْنَا مِنكُم مَّلَـٰٓئِكَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ (٦٠)

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَعِلْمٌ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَٱتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦١)
[Say,] ‘Indeed he is a portent of the Hour; so do not doubt it and follow me. This is a straight path. (61)
وَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ (٦٢)
Do not let Satan bar you [from the way of Allah]. Indeed he is your manifest enemy.’ (62)

Circumstances of Revelation

It is recorded in the Sirah of Ibn Hisham: One day, the Messenger of Allah was sitting in the mosque with Walid ibn Mughirah. Nadr ibn al-Harith also came and sat beside them, while a group of Quraysh leaders was present in the gathering. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) spoke to them, and Nadr rose to oppose him. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) refuted him with logical arguments (concerning the falsehood of idol worship), then recited this verse to them:

"إِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنْتُمْ لَها وارِدُونَ لَوْ كانَ هؤُلاءِ آلِهَةً ما وَرَدُوها وَ كُلٌّ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌..."

"Indeed, you and what you worship besides Allah are the firewood of Hell. You will [all] enter it. Had these been gods, they would not have entered it, and all will abide therein eternally."

After this incident, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) stood up and left. At that moment, Abdullah ibn al-Ziba’ra arrived and joined the group. Walid said to Abdullah: "Nadr ibn al-Harith was left helpless before Muhammad and had no answer to give. Muhammad claims that we and all our deities are the firewood of Hell!"

Abdullah said: "By Allah, if I had seen him, I would have answered him. Ask him: Is it true that all deities are in Hell along with their worshippers? If so, we worship the angels, the Jews worship Uzair, and the Christians worship Jesus, son of Mary (what harm is there if we are with the angels and Prophets like Uzair and the Messiah!)."

This response appealed to Walid and those present in the gathering, and they believed it to be a crushing argument. They conveyed this statement to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). The Messenger of Allah said: "Yes, whoever desires to be worshipped will be in Hell with his worshippers. These idolaters, in reality, worshipped the devils and whatever the devil commanded them."

It was then that the noble verse (Surah Al-Anbiya, 101) was revealed: "إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنى‌ أُولئِكَ عَنْها مُبْعَدُونَ‌": "Indeed, those for whom the best [reward] has preceded from Us (the faithful servants who never consented to be worshipped) will be kept far away from it..." and also the verse: "وَ لَمَّا ضُرِبَ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا إِذا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ‌" (the verse under discussion) was revealed. [1]

Commentary

Which Deities are in Hell?

These verses, which speak about the status of the servitude of Christ (peace be upon him) and negate the claims of the polytheists regarding his divinity and that of the idols, serve as a completion to the discussions in previous verses regarding the mission of Moses and his struggle against the Pharaonic idolaters. It is a warning to the polytheists of the Prophet's era and all polytheists of the world.

Although these verses speak concisely, through clues within the verses themselves and other verses of the Quran, their content is not complex despite the various interpretations mentioned by commentators.

First, He says: "And when the son of Mary was presented as an example, immediately your people laughed and turned away" (وَ لَمَّا ضُرِبَ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا إِذا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ‌). [1] What was this example? And who presented it regarding Jesus, son of Mary?

This is the very question over which commentators disagree, and the key to understanding the interpretation of the verse lies within it. However, careful reflection on the subsequent verses makes it clear that the "example" came from the polytheists and was stated in connection with the idols, for in the following verses we read: "ما ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلًا": "They did not present this example to you except for [the sake of] argument."

Considering this fact and what was mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, it becomes clear that the "example" refers to what the polytheists said in mockery upon hearing the noble verse: "إِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ‌": "Indeed, you and what you worship besides Allah are the firewood of Hell" (Al-Anbiya, 98). They argued that Jesus, son of Mary, was also worshipped, and according to this verse, he must be in Hell—so how wonderful that we and our idols should be neighbors with Jesus! They said this, laughed, and mocked.

Then: "They said, 'Are our gods better, or is he?'" (وَ قالُوا أَ آلِهَتُنا خَيْرٌ أَمْ هُوَ). If he is to be in Hell, our gods are not superior to him!

But know that they are aware of the truth, "and they did not present this example to you except for [the sake of] argument" (ما ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلًا).

"Rather, they are a quarrelsome and contentious people," and they resort to falsehood to obstruct the truth (بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ خَصِمُونَ‌). [1] They know well that only those deities will enter Hell who were pleased with the worship of their followers—such as Pharaoh, who invited them to worship him—not like Christ (peace be upon him), who was and is disgusted by their actions.

Rather, "He was not but a servant upon whom We bestowed favor" and whom We sent for the prophethood and guidance of the creation (إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنا عَلَيْهِ‌). "And We made him an example for the Children of Israel" (وَ جَعَلْناهُ مَثَلًا لِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ‌).

His birth from a mother without a father was one of the signs of Allah; his speaking in the cradle was another sign; and each of his miracles was a clear indication of the greatness of Allah and his status of prophethood.

Throughout his life, he confessed to the status of servitude to the Lord and invited everyone to His worship. As he himself says: "As long as I was among the nation, I did not allow anyone to deviate from the path of monotheism"; rather, the superstition of the divinity of Christ (peace be upon him) or the Trinity was created after him. [2]

Interestingly, in several narrations transmitted through both Shiite and Sunni sources, we read that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said to Ali (peace be upon him):

"In you is an example of Jesus: a people loved him and perished because of him (by exaggerating until they called him God), and a people hated him and perished because of him (like the Jews who sought to kill him). (Similarly, a group will elevate you to the status of divinity, and a group will harbor enmity toward you, and both will be in Hell). When the hypocrites heard this, they said mockingly: 'Could he find no other example for him but Jesus?' At that moment, the above verse was revealed: وَ لَمَّا ضُرِبَ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا..."

What we have mentioned above is the text of a narration that Hafiz Abu Bakr ibn Mardawayh, one of the famous Sunni scholars, recorded in the book Al-Manaqib (as cited in Kashf al-Ghummah, page 95).

The same meaning, with slight variations, was recorded by Mir Muhammad Salih Kashfi Tirmidhi in Manaqib-e Murtazavi.

A group of other Sunni scholars and great Shiite scholars have cited this incident in numerous books, sometimes without mentioning the above verse and sometimes with it. [1] The clues present in the verses indicate that this famous hadith is of the nature of "application" (tatbiq) rather than the original "circumstance of revelation" (sha'n nuzul). In other words, the original occasion for the verse was the story of Jesus and the dialogue of the Arab polytheists regarding their idols. However, because an incident similar to it occurred for Ali (peace be upon him) after that historical statement of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), the Prophet recited this verse here, as this event was, in various aspects, like an instance of it.

In the next verse, so they do not imagine that Allah needs their servitude and worship when He insists on their faith, He says: "And if We willed, We could have made instead of you angels in the earth succeeding [you]" (وَ لَوْ نَشاءُ لَجَعلْنا مِنْكُمْ مَلائِكَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ‌).

Angels who are submissive to the command of the Truth and know nothing but obedience and servitude to Him.

A group of commentators has chosen another interpretation for the verse, according to which the meaning is: "If We willed, We could have made from your offspring angels who would succeed you on earth."

Therefore, do not be surprised that Christ was born without a father; Allah is even capable of creating an angel—which is a separate species—from a human. [1] Since the birth of an angel from a human does not seem very fitting, some great commentators have interpreted it as the birth of "angel-like" humans, saying the meaning is: do not be surprised that a servant like Christ has the power to revive the dead and heal the sick by Allah's command while remaining a sincere servant obedient to His command; Allah can create from your children those who possess all the character and traits of angels. [2] However, the first interpretation is most consistent with the literal wording of the verse, and these other interpretations seem remote. [3]

The next verse points to another characteristic of Christ (peace be upon him), saying:

"And indeed, he (Jesus) is a knowledge of the Hour" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَعِلْمٌ لِلسَّاعَةِ).

Either because his birth without a father is a proof of the infinite power of Allah, in light of which the issue of life after death is resolved.

Or because the descent of Christ (peace be upon him) from heaven, according to numerous Islamic narrations, will take place at the end of time and is a sign of the approach of the Day of Resurrection.

Jabir ibn Abdullah says: I heard the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) saying:

"Jesus, son of Mary, will descend, and their leader will say: 'Come and lead us in prayer.' He will say: 'No, indeed some of you are leaders over others as an honor from Allah for this nation.'" (Then Christ will pray behind the Mahdi). [1] In another hadith from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), we read:

"How will you be when the son of Mary descends among you while your Imam is from among yourselves?" [2] In any case, applying the word "knowledge" ('ilm) to Christ is a form of emphasis and hyperbole, indicating that his descent is certainly one of the signs of the Resurrection.

The possibility has also been suggested that the pronoun in "Indeed, it" (innahu) refers to the "Quran," according to which the meaning of the verse would be: the revelation of the Quran, because it is the last of the Heavenly Books, is a proof of the proximity of the Resurrection and gives news of the standing of the Hour.

However, the context of the preceding and succeeding verses, which are about Jesus, strengthens the first interpretation.

In any case, following that, He says: the standing of the Resurrection is certain and its occurrence is near, "so do not doubt it" (فَلا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِها).

Neither in terms of belief nor in terms of action; do not behave like those heedless of the Resurrection.

"And follow Me; this is a straight path" (وَ اتَّبِعُونِ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ‌).

What path is straighter than this, which informs you of the dangerous future ahead and shows you the way of salvation from perils on the Day of Resurrection?

But Satan wants to keep you constantly in heedlessness and ignorance; be alert: "And let not Satan avert you [from the path of Allah and from mindfulness of your destiny in the Resurrection], for indeed, he is to you a clear enemy" (وَ لا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ‌).

He showed his enmity and hostility from the first day, once when tempting your parents Adam and Eve and causing their expulsion from Paradise, and another time when he swore that he would mislead all the children of Adam except the "sincere ones." How can you remain silent before such a sworn enemy and allow him to dominate your heart and soul, and turn you away from the path of truth with his constant whisperings?!
