He created him from dust; then He said to him, 'Be,' and he was." (If Jesus was born without a father, it is not a cause for wonder; Adam was born from dust by Allah's command without a father or mother). 3- Others have suggested that the "example" was the statement of the polytheists who said: if the Christians worship Christ, why should our deities, who are superior to him, not be worthy of worship? However, attention to the specifics raised in the above verses shows that none of these three interpretations are correct because the verses clearly show: first, the example was from the polytheists; second, it was a controversial matter and, in their view, laughable; third, it was something contrary to the status of Christ's (peace be upon him) servitude; and fourth, it served their goal of arguing over a false matter. These characteristics are only consistent with the interpretation we provided in the text. [^1]: For more information, refer to the book Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, p. 398 onwards, Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 609 onwards, and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed. [^1]: Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, Abu al-Futuh Razi, and some others chose the first interpretation. However, the second interpretation was cited by Qurtubi, Fakhr Razi, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, and Maraghi as either the only meaning or one of two meanings. [^2]: Al-Mizan, under the verse discussed. [^3]: According to the first interpretation, "min" is for "substitution" (badaliyyah), and according to the second and third interpretations, "min" is "originative" (nashwiyyah). [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan cited this hadith from Sahih Muslim under the verses discussed. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed, and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 25, p. 88.

Ayahs

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ عِيسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (٦٣)
When Jesus brought those manifest proofs, he said, ‘I have certainly brought you wisdom, and [I have come] to make clear to you some of the things that you differ about. So be wary of Allah and obey me. (63)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ (٦٤)
Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path.’ (64)
فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ (٦٥)
But the factions differed among themselves. So woe to the wrongdoers for the punishment of a painful day. (65)

Commentary

Those Who Exaggerated Regarding the Messiah

In the preceding verses, some aspects of the life of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام were mentioned, and the current verses continue that discussion, specifically emphasizing Prophet Jesus's call to pure monotheism and the rejection of any form of polytheism.

First, it states: "When Jesus came with clear proofs (miracles and divine signs), he said: 'I have come to you with wisdom and to clarify for you some of those matters in which you differ'" (وَلَمَّا جَاءَ عِيسَىٰ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ).

Thus, Jesus's primary assets were "clear proofs," meaning divine signs and miracles that, on one hand, demonstrated his truthfulness and, on the other, clarified truths regarding the Origin (Allah), the Resurrection, and the needs of human life.

In this phrase, Prophet Jesus عليه السلام describes the content of his call as "wisdom" (hikmah). We know that the linguistic root of "wisdom" means "to prevent something for the sake of its rectification." Subsequently, it has been applied to all true beliefs and correct life programs that prevent a human from any deviation in faith and action, and which deal with the purification of his soul and ethics. Therefore, "wisdom" here has a broad meaning that encompasses both "theoretical wisdom" and "practical wisdom."

In addition to these, this wisdom pursues another goal: resolving the differences that disrupt the social order and leave people bewildered and helpless. Hence, Prophet Jesus emphasizes this issue in the core of his speech.

A question arises here that most commentators have addressed:

Question

Why does he say: "I have come to clarify some of the matters in which you differ"? Why does he not clarify all of them?

Answer

Several answers have been given to this question, the most appropriate of which is:

The differences people have are of two types: some affect their destiny in terms of belief and action, and regarding the individual and society; while another part consists of differences in matters that are never destiny-shaping, such as various theories about the origin of the solar system and the heavens, the nature of the celestial spheres and stars, the essence of the human soul, the reality of life, and the like.

It is clear that Prophets are commissioned to end differences in the first category by clarifying realities, but they are never commissioned to end every single difference, especially those that have no impact on human destiny.

There is also the possibility that clarifying "some" differences is the result and ultimate goal of the Prophets' call; meaning they eventually succeed in resolving some differences, but resolving all differences in this world is not possible. For this reason, in several verses of the Holy Quran, one of the characteristics of the Day of Resurrection is described as the ending of all differences. In Surah An-Nahl, verse 92, we read: وَلَيُبَيِّنَنَّ لَكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ مَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ: "He will surely clarify for you on the Day of Resurrection that wherein you used to differ." (The same meaning appears in Surah Al-Imran 55, Al-Ma'idah 48, Al-An'am 164, Al-Hajj 69, and others). [1]

[1]: Some other commentators have said that "some" (ba'd) here means "all"! Or that the expression "some of that which you differ in" is the addition of the described to the description. Or that this expression indicates: "I only clarify matters of religion for you, not your differences in worldly affairs." However, none of these interpretations are noteworthy.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Now that this is the case and this is the content of my call, fear Allah and obey me" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ).

Then, to remove any ambiguity regarding his own servitude, he says: "Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ). It is noteworthy that the word "Lord" (Rabb) is repeated twice in this verse—once regarding himself and once regarding the people—to make it clear: "You and I are equal, and your Lord and my Lord is One."

I too, in my entire existence and being, am in need of a Creator and Disposer just like you; He is my Owner and my Guide.

To emphasize further, he adds: "So worship Him" (فَاعْبُدُوهُ).

For none other than Him is worthy of worship; all are nurtured (marbub) and He is the Lord (Rabb), and all are owned and He is the Owner.

Again, he emphasizes his words with another sentence so that no excuse remains, saying: "This is the straight path" (هَذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ). [1] Yes, the straight path is the path of servitude and being a servant to the Lord; it is a path in which there is no deviation or crookedness, just as it appears in Surah Ya-Sin, verse 61:

وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هَذَا صِرَاطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ: "Did I not covenant with you... that you should worship Me? This is the straight path."

[1]: A similar verse with a slight difference appears in Surah Maryam verse 36 and Surah Al-An'am verse 51. The repetition of this meaning is an emphasis on the fact that Jesus established the final proof (itmam al-hujjah) to all of them regarding his own servitude.

However, it is strange that despite all these emphases, "among the many groups that emerged after Jesus عليه السلام, factions arose who differed concerning Jesus" (فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزَابُ مِن بَيْنِهِمْ). [1] Some imagined him to be God who had descended to earth! Others called him the son of God. Some considered him one of the three persons (the three holy essences: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit).

Only a group that was in the minority considered him a servant of Allah and His messenger, but eventually, the opinion of the majority prevailed, and the issue of the Trinity and triple gods permeated the Christian world.

In this regard, an interesting historical hadith has been narrated, which we have cited in Volume 13, page 70, under verse 36 of Surah Maryam.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that the difference was not only among Christians, but rather differences arose between the Jews and the followers of Christ regarding him. His followers exaggerated about him and raised him to the rank of divinity, while his enemies accused him and his chaste mother Maryam of the worst accusations. Such is the way of the ignorant: one group in excess (ifrat) and another in deficiency (tafrit). Or as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said: some are "exaggerating lovers" (muhibb ghal) and some are "hateful detractors" (mubghid qal), when he said:

"Two groups perished concerning me: the exaggerating lovers who thought I was God, and the accusing enemies who attributed false things to me!" [1] And how similar are the circumstances of these two great figures! At the end of the verse, He threatens them with the painful punishment of the Day of Resurrection, saying:

"Woe to those who did wrong and deviated from the straight path; woe to them from the punishment of a painful day" (فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ). [2] Yes, the Day of Resurrection is a painful day: the length of its reckoning is painful, its punishment and penalty are painful, its regret and sorrow are painful, and its disgrace and scandal are also painful.

[1]: The pronoun in "among them" (baynihim) refers to those whom the Messiah عليه السلام addressed in the previous verse and invited to the servitude of Allah.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 117.

[2]: Note that "painful" (alim) is an adjective for "day" (yawm), not for "punishment" (adhab).

Ayahs

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٦٦)
Do they not consider that the Hour may overtake them suddenly while they are unaware? (66)
ٱلْأَخِلَّآءُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٦٧)
On that day, friends will be one another’s enemies, except for the Godwary. (67)
يَـٰعِبَادِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ (٦٨)
[They will be told,] ‘O My servants! Today you will have no fear, nor will you grieve (68)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ (٦٩)
—those who believed in Our signs and had been Muslims. (69)

Commentary

What Are You Waiting For? Except the Punishment of the Hereafter?

In the preceding verses, the discussion focused on the stubborn idolaters, as well as the deviants and polytheists among the followers of 'Isa (Jesus) عليه السلام. In the verses under discussion, their ultimate end is depicted.

First, it says: "Are they waiting for anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly while they are unaware?" (هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ).

This question, posed as a rhetorical negation, actually describes the true state of such individuals. It is like when we say of a person who refuses to listen to the advice of any sincere counselor and brings about his own destruction with his own hands: "He is only waiting for his own death!" The term "Hour" (al-Sa'ah) in this verse, as in many other verses of the Quran, refers to the Day of Resurrection, because its events occur with such speed that it is as if everything happens in a single hour.

Of course, this word is sometimes used to mean the moment of the end of the world as well, and since there is not much distance between the two, this expression may encompass both stages.

In any case, the arrival of the Resurrection, which begins with the sudden end of the world, is described with two attributes in the above verse: first, its "suddenness" (baghtatan), and second, the general public's lack of awareness regarding the time of its occurrence.

It is possible for something to happen suddenly while we were expecting it beforehand and had prepared ourselves to face its difficulties; however, the true misfortune is when an extraordinarily severe and crushing event occurs suddenly while we are completely heedless of it.

The state of the criminals is exactly like this; they are caught so off-guard that, according to some narrations from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"The Hour will be established while two men are milking a ewe, and two men are folding a garment," then he صلى الله عليه وآله recited: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ.

He said: "The Resurrection will occur suddenly while (everyone is busy with their daily life); men are busy milking sheep, and other men are busy spreading fabric (and discussing buying and selling)." Then the Prophet recited the above verse: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ [1]. How painful it is for a human to be so blindsided by such an event from which there is no return, and to be submerged in its waves without any preparation.

Then, the verse unveils the state of friends who joined hands in the path of sin and corruption, or the glitz and glamour of the world, saying: "Close friends on that Day will be enemies to one another, except for the righteous!" (الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ) [2]. This verse, which is a description of the scenes of the Resurrection, clearly shows that the "Hour" in the previous verse also refers to the Day of Judgment—the day when bonds of friendship are severed, except for those bonds established for the sake of Allah and in His name.

The transformation of such friendships into enmity on that Day is natural, because each of them considers the other the cause of their misery and wretchedness: "It was you who showed me this path and invited me to it; it was you who beautified the world in my eyes and encouraged me toward it; yes, it was you who drowned me in heedlessness and pride and kept me unaware of my fate." Each of them says such things to the other.

Only the righteous are those whose bond of friendship is eternal, because it revolves around eternal values, and its fruitful results become more apparent in the Resurrection, strengthening that bond further.

Naturally, friends help each other in the affairs of life. If friendship is based on evil and corruption, they are partners in each other's crimes; and if it is based on goodness and righteousness, they are partners in each other's rewards. Therefore, it is not surprising that friendship of the first kind turns into enmity there, and the second kind into a firmer friendship.

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Know that every friendship (khullah) that existed in the world for other than Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, will turn into enmity on the Day of Resurrection." [1]

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation of the attributes and states of the "righteous" (muttaqin) and a statement of their glorious destiny.

On that Day, Allah says to them: "O My servants! No fear shall there be on you this Day, nor shall you grieve" (يا عِبادِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَ لا أَنْتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ).

What a wonderful message! A message without an intermediary from Allah; a message that begins with the best of descriptions: "O My servants!"; a message that eliminates the most significant human anxieties on that day of great anxiety; a message that wipes away any grief or sorrow regarding the past from the heart. Yes, this message possesses the four aforementioned merits.

In the final verse under discussion, these righteous and noble servants are further identified with two other phrases, stating: "Those who believed in Our signs and were submissive [to Our command]" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِآياتِنا وَ كانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ).

Yes, it is they who are addressed with such a glorious address and are included in such blessings.

In reality, the two phrases above are an eloquent definition of their belief and action: "Faith" (iman) clarifies their firm ideological foundations, and "Islam" clarifies their submission in action to the command of Truth and the implementation of His instructions.

[^1]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan", Vol. 25, p. 89. [^2]: "Akhilla'" is the plural of "Khalil," from the root "khullah," meaning affection and friendship. Its original root is "khalal" (on the meter of sharaf), meaning the space between two bodies; and since love and friendship seemingly penetrate into the layers of the human heart, this word is used for it. [^1]: Tafsir "Ali ibn Ibrahim," as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 612.

Ayahs

ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ أَنتُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ تُحْبَرُونَ (٧٠)
Enter paradise, you and your spouses, rejoicing.’ (70)
يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِصِحَافٍ مِّن ذَهَبٍ وَأَكْوَابٍ ۖ وَفِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِيهِ ٱلْأَنفُسُ وَتَلَذُّ ٱلْأَعْيُنُ ۖ وَأَنتُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٧١)
They will be served around with golden dishes and goblets, and therein will be whatever the souls desire and eyes delight in. ‘You will remain in it [forever]. (71)
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٧٢)
That is the paradise you have been given to inherit for what you used to do. (72)
لَكُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ مِّنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٧٣)
There are abundant fruits for you in it, from which you will eat.’ (73)

Commentary

Whatever the heart desires and the eye delights in...

These verses describe the rewards of the sincere servants of Allah and the righteous believers who were characterized in the preceding verses, promising them the eternal Paradise with seven precious blessings.

First, it states that they are addressed by Allah, the Great and the Bestower: "Enter Paradise" (ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ).

In this manner, the true host is Allah Himself, who invites His guests, saying: "Please, enter Paradise!" Then, referring to the first blessing, He adds: "You and your spouses" (أَنْتُمْ وَ أَزْواجُكُمْ‌).

It is clear that being alongside faithful and kind spouses is delightful for both men and their wives; if they were partners in the sorrows of the world, they shall also be partners in the joy of the Hereafter.

Some have interpreted "spouses" (azwaj) here to mean peers, friends, and close associates, which would also be a great blessing, but the apparent meaning of the verse is the former.

Then He adds: "Be filled with joy and happiness, such that the effects of this delight radiate from your faces" (تُحْبَرُونَ‌).

"Tuhbarun" is derived from the root "habr" (rhyming with fikr), meaning a desirable effect. It is sometimes applied to adornment and the signs of joy that appear on the face. If scholars are called "ahbar" (plural of hibr, rhyming with abr), it is because of the effects they leave behind within human societies, as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said:

العلماء باقون ما بقى الدهر اعيانهم مفقودة و امثالهم فى القلوب موجوده‌

"The scholars remain as long as time exists; their physical persons are lost, but their examples exist within the hearts." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 147.

Regarding the third blessing, He says: "Trays of food and cups of pure wine made of gold, filled with the best heavenly food and drink, shall be circulated around them by special servants" (يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِصِحافٍ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ وَ أَكْوابٍ‌).

They are served in the finest vessels, with the best food, in ultimate peace, comfort, and purity, without any trouble.

"Sihaf" is the plural of "sahfah" (rhyming with safhah), originally derived from the root "sahf" meaning to spread out; here it refers to large, wide platters.

"Akwab" is the plural of "kub," referring to water vessels that do not have a handle, which in modern terms would be a "goblet" or "chalice."

Although the above verse only mentions golden vessels and does not explicitly discuss the food and drinks within them, it is obvious that empty vessels are never circulated around guests for hospitality.

In the fourth and fifth stages, He points to two other blessings in which all the material and spiritual blessings of the world are gathered, saying: "In Paradise, there is whatever the souls desire and the eyes delight in" (وَ فِيها ما تَشْتَهِيهِ الْأَنْفُسُ وَ تَلَذُّ الْأَعْيُنُ‌).

As the late Tabarsi stated in Majma' al-Bayan, if all of creation were to gather to describe the types of heavenly blessings, they would never be able to add anything to what is contained in this sentence.

What expression could be more beautiful and comprehensive than this? An expression as vast as existence itself, and as wide as what our minds can or cannot conceive today—an expression beyond which no other can be found.

Interestingly, the matter of the "heart's desire" is mentioned separately from the "delight of the eye," and this separation is full of meaning:

Is this a case of mentioning the specific after the general? This would be because the "delight of sight" holds extraordinary importance, being superior to other pleasures. Or is it because the phrase "whatever the souls desire" describes the pleasures of taste, smell, hearing, and touch, while the phrase "the eyes delight in" describes the pleasure of sight?

Some also believe that the phrase "whatever the souls desire" refers to all physical pleasures, while "the eyes delight in" refers to spiritual pleasures. What pleasure in Paradise is higher than for a human to look upon the peerless Beauty of the Lord with the eye of the heart? A single moment of this is superior to all the material blessings of Paradise.

Naturally, the greater the longing for the Beloved, the greater the pleasure of the meeting.

Question

A question has been raised by commentators: Does the generality and breadth of this verse's concept imply that if they request things that were forbidden in this world, Allah will grant them?

Answer

Raising this question is actually due to overlooking one point: that prohibitions and evils are, in reality, like unsuitable food for the human soul. Surely, a healthy soul does not crave such food; it is the diseased souls that sometimes incline toward toxins and unsuitable nourishment.

We see patients who, during their illness, even develop a desire to eat soil or other such things, but as soon as the illness is removed, that false appetite vanishes. Indeed, the inhabitants of Paradise will never feel an inclination toward such acts, because the soul's attraction toward them is a characteristic of the diseased souls of the people of Hell.

This question is similar to what has been narrated in a Hadith: a Bedouin came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked, "Will camels be found in Paradise? For I am very fond of camels!"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, knowing that there are blessings there that will make the Bedouin forget his camel, replied with a short and meaningful phrase:

يا اعرابى ان ادخلك اللَّه الجنة اصبت فيها ما اشتهت نفسك و لذت عينك!:

"O Bedouin! If Allah enters you into Paradise, you will find therein whatever your heart desires and your eye delights in seeing!" [^1] In other words, that is a world where man becomes completely harmonized with realities, and as the poet says: "Whatever you see, your heart will desire! And whatever your heart desires, you shall see!"

In any case, since the value of a blessing is when it is eternal, in the sixth description, He puts the inhabitants of Paradise at ease in this regard, saying: "You shall abide therein forever" (وَ أَنْتُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌).

Lest the thought of the blessing's cessation trouble their minds or make them anxious about the future.

Here, to clarify that all these heavenly blessings are given as a "price" (for deeds) and not merely as an "excuse," He adds: "This is the Paradise you are made to inherit because of the deeds you used to do" (وَ تِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي أُورِثْتُمُوها بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

It is interesting that, on one hand, He mentions the recompense for deeds, and on the other hand, He uses the term "inheritance" (irth), which is usually used for a blessing that reaches a person without heartache, effort, or toil. This indicates that your deeds are the primary foundation of your salvation, but what you receive is so superior in comparison to your deeds that it is as if you obtained it all for free from Divine Grace!

[^1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 391.

Some also consider this expression to refer to what we have previously mentioned: that every human has a dwelling in Paradise and a place in Hell; the inhabitants of Paradise inherit the dwellings of the inhabitants of Hell, and the inhabitants of Hell inherit the dwellings of the inhabitants of Paradise! However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In the final and seventh blessing, the talk is of heavenly fruits, which are among the best of Divine blessings. He says: "For you therein is abundant fruit from which you eat" (لَكُمْ فِيها فاكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ مِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ‌).

In truth, the trays and cups represented various types of food and drink, but fruits have a separate account of their own, which is mentioned in the final verse under discussion.

Interestingly, with the expression "from which" (minha), He conveys the reality that heavenly fruits are so abundant that you only eat a portion of them; thus, it is an inexhaustible collection, and its trees are always laden and productive.

In a Hadith, it is stated:

لا ينزع رجل فى الجنة ثمرة من ثمرها الا نبت مثلاها مكانها:

"No man in Paradise plucks a fruit from its trees except that two like it grow in its place." [^1] These are part of the soul-nourishing heavenly blessings that await those who possess clear faith and worthy, righteous deeds.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 392.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ فِى عَذَابِ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ (٧٤)
Indeed the guilty will remain [forever] in the punishment of hell. (74)
لَا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ وَهُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ (٧٥)
It will not be lightened for them, and they will be despondent in it. (75)
وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٧٦)
We did not wrong them, but they themselves were wrongdoers. (76)
وَنَادَوْا۟ يَـٰمَـٰلِكُ لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنَا رَبُّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّكُم مَّـٰكِثُونَ (٧٧)
They will call out, ‘O Malik! Let your Lord finish us off!’ He will say, ‘Indeed you will stay on.’ (77)
لَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٧٨)
‘We certainly brought you the truth, but most of you were averse to the truth.’ (78)
أَمْ أَبْرَمُوٓا۟ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ (٧٩)
Have they settled on some [devious] plan? Indeed, We too are settling [on Our plans]. (79)
أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لَا نَسْمَعُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَىٰهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَرُسُلُنَا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ (٨٠)
Do they suppose that We do not hear their secret thoughts and their secret talks? Yes indeed [We do]! And with them are Our messengers, writing down [everything]. (80)

Commentary

We Wish to Die and Be Relieved from the Torment!

In these verses, the fate of the criminals and disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection is described so that, by comparison with the inspiring destiny of the believers who obeyed their Lord, both dimensions of the matter become clearer.

First, He says: "Indeed, the criminals will remain eternally in the punishment of Hell" (إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي عَذابِ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدُونَ). The word "Mujrim" (criminal) is derived from the root "Jurm," which originally means "to cut." It is used for cutting fruit from a tree, as well as cutting down the trees themselves, but it was later applied to performing any kind of evil deed—perhaps because such acts "cut" or sever a human being from Allah and from human values.

However, it is certain that "all criminals" are not meant here; rather, it refers to those criminals who have chosen the path of disbelief (Kufr). This is evidenced by the mention of "Khulud" (eternity) and everlasting punishment, as well as the contrast with the believers discussed in the preceding verses. The suggestion by some commentators that this includes all criminals is highly improbable.

But since it is possible that "eternal punishment" might be alleviated over time or gradually decrease in intensity, the next verse adds: "The punishment will never be lightened for them, and there is no way of escape for them; they will be in utter despair therein" (لا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ وَ هُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ).

Thus, their punishment is permanent both in terms of duration and intensity, because "Futur," as Al-Raghib states, means stillness after heat, softness after severity, and weakness after strength.

"Mublis" is derived from "Iblas," which originally refers to the grief that overcomes a person due to extreme distress. Since such grief invites a person to silence, the root "Iblas" is also used to mean silence and being unable to provide an answer. Furthermore, because in severe hardships a person loses hope of salvation, this root is used for despair (Ya’s) as well. The naming of "Iblis" (Satan) by this name is due to this very meaning—that he is despaired of Allah's mercy.

In any case, these two verses emphasize three points: the issue of eternity, the lack of alleviation of punishment, and absolute grief and despair. How painful is a punishment that is compounded by these three factors!

In the following verse, He points out that this painful Divine punishment is something they have brought upon themselves, saying: "And We did not wrong them, but it was they who were the wrongdoers" (وَ ما ظَلَمْناهُمْ وَ لكِنْ كانُوا هُمُ الظَّالِمِينَ). In reality, just as the previous verses identified the source of all those endless blessings as the deeds of the God-fearing believers, here too, the source of this eternal punishment is attributed to their own actions.

What injustice and wrongdoing could be greater than a person denying the Divine signs and striking at the very root of their own salvation? "And who is more unjust than he who invents a lie against Allah..." (Surah As-Saff, 7).

Indeed, the Quran considers the primary source of all happiness and wretchedness to be the human being himself and his actions, not the imaginary issues that some groups have fabricated for themselves.

Then, describing another aspect of their misery, it says: "And they will cry out: 'O Malik (the keeper of Hell)! We wish your Lord would put an end to us (let us die) so that we may be relieved from this painful torment!'" (وَ نادَوْا يا مالِكُ لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنا رَبُّكَ). Although everyone flees from death and desires the continuation of life, sometimes calamities press so hard upon a person that they plead with God for death. This is something that might happen to a few in this world, but there, for the criminals, it is a universal state, and they all plead for death.

But what use is it? For Malik, the keeper of Hell, answers them: "Indeed, you are to remain!" There is no salvation, not even through death (قالَ إِنَّكُمْ ماكِثُونَ) ^1. Remarkably, according to some commentators, Malik gives this answer with utter indifference after a thousand years! How painful is this disregard [^2]. It might be asked: what kind of request is this when they are certain there is no death? However, it should be noted that such requests—

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses in question. Of course, some have cited the number of intervening years as one hundred or forty; whatever it may be, it is evidence of indifference.

—are natural for a wretched person who has lost hope in everything. Yes, when they see all paths of salvation closed to them, such a cry rises from their hearts! But why do they not ask for death directly from Allah, but instead tell Malik to ask his Lord to grant them death? Because on that day, they are veiled from their Lord, as we read in verse 15 of Surah Al-Mutaffifin: "Nay! Surely, they will be veiled from their Lord that Day." Therefore, they present their request through the angel of punishment, or perhaps because he is an angel and is close to Allah.

In the next verse, which is effectively the reason for their eternity in the fire of Hell, it says: "We certainly brought you the Truth, but most of you were averse to the Truth and would not submit to it" (لَقَدْ جِئْناكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كارِهُونَ).

Commentators have expressed two different views on whether these words are spoken by Malik (where "We" refers to the assembly of angels, of which Malik is one) or if it is the speech of Allah.

While the flow of the discourse suggests it is a continuation of Malik's speech, the content of the verse itself is more consistent with the speech of Allah. Another piece of evidence for this is verse 73 of Surah Az-Zumar: "And its keepers will say to them: 'Did there not come to you messengers from among yourselves, reciting to you the verses of your Lord?'"

Here, the keepers of Hell consider the messengers to be the bringers of Truth, not themselves.

The term "Al-Haqq" (The Truth) has a broad meaning that encompasses all destiny-shaping realities, though primarily it refers to Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the Quran.

This expression is actually an indication that you were not merely opposed to the Prophets of Allah; rather, you were fundamentally opposed to the Truth itself, and this opposition has brought you eternal punishment.

In the following verse, a glimpse of their hatred and aversion toward the Truth and their support for falsehood is reflected, saying: "Or have they devised a firm plan (to plot)? Then We too are firm in Our resolve" (أَمْ أَبْرَمُوا أَمْراً فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ) ^1. They hatched plots to extinguish the light of Islam, to assassinate the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and to strike the Muslims in any way they could.

We too have resolved to punish them severely in this world and the next.

Some commentators consider the occasion of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) for this verse to be the plot to assassinate the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) before the Hijrah, which is also mentioned in the verse: "And [remember] when those who disbelieved plotted against you..." (Al-Anfal, 30) [^2]. However, it appears that this is a matter of application (Tatbiq) rather than a specific occasion of revelation.

The next verse explains one of the reasons for their plotting, saying: "Or do they think that We do not hear their secrets and their private conversations?" (أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لا نَسْمَعُ سِرَّهُمْ وَ نَجْواهُمْ).

But it is not so; both We hear, and "Our messengers (angels) are with them, recording their secrets and private talks" (بَلى‌ وَ رُسُلُنا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ).

"Sirr" (secret) refers to a matter that a person hides in their heart or only—

^1: "Am" in the above verse is munqati'ah (disjunctive) and means "Nay, but." "Ibram" means twisting and making firm.

[^2]: Fakhr al-Razi, under the verses in question.

—discusses with their trusted friends, and "Najwa" refers to whispered conversations.

Yes, Allah not only hears their quiet words spoken in secret meetings or whispers, but He is also aware of the internal monologue and the dialogue they have within their own hearts, for there is no difference between the hidden and the manifest for Him. The angels assigned to record the deeds and words of humans also continuously record these words in their book of deeds—even though the realities are clear without it—so that they may face the retribution for their actions, words, and plots in this world and the Hereafter.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِن كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْعَـٰبِدِينَ (٨١)
Say, ‘If the All-beneficent had offspring, I would have been the first to worship [him].’ (81)
سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٨٢)
Clear is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, the Lord of the Throne, of whatever they allege [concerning Him]! (82)
فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٨٣)
So leave them to gossip and play until they encounter the day they are promised. (83)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِلَـٰهٌ وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَـٰهٌ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٨٤)
It is He who is God in the sky, and God on the earth, and He is the All-Wise, the All-Knowing. (84)
وَتَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَعِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (٨٥)
Blessed is He to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and with Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and to Him you will be brought back. (85)

Commentary

Let Them Plunge into Their Falsehood

Since the previous verses, especially at the beginning of the Surah, discussed the Arab polytheists and their belief in God having offspring—imagining angels to be the daughters of Allah—and since a few verses prior, the discourse turned to Messiah (peace be upon him) and his call to pure monotheism and servitude to the Lord, the verses under discussion approach the negation of these false beliefs from another angle, stating: "Say [O Prophet] to those who speak of the existence of a son for Allah: If the Most Compassionate had a son, I would be the first to honor and obey him" (قُلْ إِنْ كانَ لِلرَّحْمنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْعابِدِينَ).

This is because my faith and belief in Allah are greater than all of yours, and my knowledge and awareness are more profound; therefore, I would have preceded you in honoring His son and obeying him.

Although the content of this verse has appeared complex to a group of commentators, leading them to various justifications—some of which seem quite strange [1]—the actual message of the verse is not complex at all. This is a fascinating method used when dealing with stubborn individuals. It is like saying to someone who mistakenly claims a certain person is the most learned (while that person possesses no knowledge): "If he were the most learned, I would be the first to follow him." This forces the claimant into reflection, prompting them to seek evidence for their claim, and eventually, when they hit a dead end, they may wake up from their state of negligence.

However, two points must be noted here:

First: The word 'ibadah (worship/servitude) does not always mean "worship" in the sense of prayer; sometimes it means obedience, veneration, and respect, and here it carries this meaning. For, even on the impossible assumption that Allah had a son, there would be no reason to worship that son; however, since he would—by this impossible assumption—be the son of Allah, he would necessarily deserve respect and obedience.

Second: In Arabic linguistics, the word law (if) is usually used in such cases to denote impossibility. If it was not used in the verse under discussion, it was only for the sake of mumashat (conceding for the sake of argument) and aligning the speech with the opposing party's perspective.

In this way, the Holy Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) says, to assure them that it is impossible for Allah to have a son: "If He had a son, I would have honored him before you."

[1] For instance, some commentators have taken in to mean negation and ana awwalul-'abidin to mean "the first worshiper of Allah," according to which the meaning would be: "Allah never has a son, and I am the first worshiper of Allah!" Others have taken 'abidin to mean "those who refuse," meaning: "If God had a son, I would never worship such a god, because by having a son He could not be God." It is clear that such interpretations are in no way consistent with the literal appearance of the verse.

After this statement, the text proceeds to a clear proof for negating these vain claims, saying: "Glory be to the Lord of the heavens and the earth, the Lord of the Throne, from what they describe!" (سُبْحانَ رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ).

He who is the Owner and Disposer of the heavens and the earth, and the Lord of the Great Throne, what need does He have for a son? He is an infinite Being encompassing the entire universe and the Sustainer of all creation. A son is necessary for one who dies, so that his lineage may continue through the child.

A son is necessary for one who needs help and companionship in times of weakness and loneliness.

Ultimately, the existence of a son is evidence of being physical and being confined within the limits of time and space.

The Lord of the Throne, the heavens, and the earth, who is sanctified and pure from all these attributes, has no need for a son.

The expression "Lord of the Throne" after "Lord of the heavens and the earth" is, in fact, a mention of the general after the specific. For the "Throne" ('Arsh), as we have previously stated, refers to the entire realm of existence which is the seat of the Great Allah's sovereignty.

It is also possible that "Throne" refers to the metaphysical world (the world beyond nature) in contrast to the "heavens and earth" which refer to the material world.

(For further explanation regarding the meaning of the Throne, refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 200, under verse 255, and more extensively in Volume 20, under verse 7 of Surah Al-Mu'min).

Then, as a gesture of indifference and a threat to these stubborn people—which is another method of debating such individuals—it adds: "Now that it is so, leave them to plunge [into their falsehood] and play, until they meet their Day which they are promised" (and reap the bitter fruits of their ugly and shameful deeds and thoughts) (فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَ يَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ).

It is clear that the "promised day" refers to the Day of Resurrection. The suggestion by some that it refers to the moment of death seems very unlikely, as the recompense for deeds occurs in the Resurrection, not at the moment of death.

This is the same "Promised Day" sworn by in Surah Al-Buruj, verse 2: "وَ الْيَوْمِ الْمَوْعُودِ" ("By the Promised Day" [the Day of Resurrection]).

The next two verses continue the discourse on the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid), which from one perspective is a conclusion to the previous verses and from another is a proof to strengthen and complete them. In these verses, seven attributes are mentioned for Allah, all of which are effective in strengthening the foundations of Monotheism.

First, in response to the polytheists who believed in separate gods and deities for the heavens and the earth—and even fabricated gods of the sea, gods of the desert, gods of war, and gods of peace, and various gods according to the types of beings in their imagination—He says: "He is the One who is God in the heaven and God in the earth" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّماءِ إِلهٌ وَ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلهٌ).

For by accepting His Lordship (Rububiyyah) over the heavens and the earth, which was mentioned in the previous verses, the issue of Divinity (Aluhiyyah) is also proven; because the true Deity is the one who is the Lord, Manager, and Disposer of the universe.

Neither the "lords of species," nor the angels, nor Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him), nor the idols—none are worthy of worship and servitude, for they do not possess the station of Lordship. They, in turn, are created, sustained, and are guests at His table of provision; they too worship Him.

Then, in the second and third descriptions, it adds: "And He is the All-Wise, the All-Knowing" (وَ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ).

All His actions are based on calculation and wisdom, and He is aware and informed of everything. Thus, He knows the deeds of His servants well and rewards or punishes them according to His wisdom.

In the fourth and fifth descriptions, it speaks of the abundant and permanent blessings of His existence and His ownership of the heavens and the earth, saying: "Blessed and Everlasting is He to whom belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them" (وَ تَبارَكَ الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما).

Tabaraka is derived from the root barakah, meaning possessing abundant good, or stability and permanence, or both. Both apply to Allah, for His existence is eternal and stable, and He is the source of abundant blessings.

Fundamentally, abundant good without stability is not a complete concept, for no matter how abundant goods and virtues are, if they are temporary and fleeting, they are not truly "abundant."

Finally, in the sixth and seventh descriptions, it adds: "And with Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and to Him you shall be returned" (وَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ وَ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ).

Therefore, if you seek good and blessing, seek it from Him, not from idols. Your destiny in the Resurrection is in His hands, and your return on that day is to Allah alone; idols and other deities have no role in these matters.

Key Points

  1. In these verses, "heavens and earth" (samawat wa ard) is repeated three times: once to express the Lordship of the Lord and His management and disposal; once to express the Divinity of the Lord; and once to express His ownership and sovereignty. These three are interrelated and are, in fact, cause and effect of one another: He is the "Owner," therefore He is the "Lord," and consequently He is the "God." Describing Him as "All-Wise" and "All-Knowing" is a completion of these meanings.
  1. It is understood from some Islamic narrations that the expression in the above verses, "هُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّماءِ إِلهٌ وَ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلهٌ", was used as a pretext by some heretics and polytheists. Through sophistry, they interpreted it to mean there is one deity in the heaven and another deity on the earth, whereas the verse says the exact opposite: He is the One who is the Deity both in the heaven and on the earth—meaning the Deity everywhere is Him.

Nevertheless, when this matter was presented as a question to the Infallible Imams, they answered them with both counter-arguments and explanatory proofs.

Among these, it is narrated in the book al-Kafi from Hisham ibn al-Hakam that Abu Shakir al-Dayasani [1] said to me: "There is a verse in the Quran that supports our view!" I asked: "Which verse?" He said: "This verse: هُوَ الَّذِي فِي السَّماءِ إِلهٌ وَ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلهٌ." I did not know how to answer him.

[1] Abu Shakir al-Dayasani was one of the scholars of the Dayasaniyyah sect, who held dualistic beliefs and believed in a god of light and a god of darkness (Dehkhoda Encyclopedia, entry: Dayasan).

That year, I was honored to perform the pilgrimage to the House of Allah and came to Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) and related the incident. He said: "This is the speech of a wicked atheist. When you return, ask him: 'What is your name in Kufa?' He will say: 'So-and-so.' Ask: 'What is your name in Basra?' He will say: 'So-and-so.' Then say: 'Our Lord is likewise; in the heaven He is the God and Deity, and on the earth He is the God and Deity, and likewise in the seas, in the deserts, and in every place, He is the God and Deity.'"

Hisham says: "When I returned, I went to Abu Shakir and gave him this answer. He said: 'This speech is not from you; you have brought this from the Hijaz!'" [1] The great commentator Tabarsi mentioned two reasons for the repetition of the word "God" (Ilah) in the verse under discussion: one is the emphasis on the Divinity of the Lord everywhere, and the other is an indication that the angels of heaven worship Him and the humans on earth also worship Him; therefore, He is the Deity of angels, humans, and all beings in the earth and the heavens.

[1] Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, Book of Monotheism, "Chapter on Movement and Transition," Hadith 10.

Ayahs

وَلَا يَمْلِكُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٨٦)
Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who are witness to the truth and who know [for whom to intercede]. (86)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٨٧)
If you ask them, ‘Who created them?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Then where do they stray? (87)
وَقِيلِهِۦ يَـٰرَبِّ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
And his plaint: ‘My Lord! Indeed these are a people who will not have faith!’ (88)
فَٱصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ (٨٩)
So disregard them, and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know. (89)

Commentary

Who is Capable of Intercession?

In these verses, which are the final verses of Surah Az-Zukhruf, the discourse continues regarding the invalidation of the creed of polytheism and the bitter end of the polytheists. It reveals the falsehood of their beliefs through other proofs. First, It states: If they turn to these deities with the hope of intercession, they must know that "those whom they invoke besides Him possess no power of intercession" (وَ لا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ).

"Intercession" in the presence of Allah is only by His permission and command, and the All-Wise Allah has never given such permission or command to these worthless stones and woods that lack intellect and perception.

However, since among their deities there were angels and the like, the end of the verse excludes them, stating: "except those who bear witness to the truth" (إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

Those who have accepted the Oneness and Unity of Allah in all stages and are completely submissive to the Truth—yes, this group possesses the power of intercession by the permission of the Lord.

But it is not the case that they will intercede for anyone, even if they are idol-worshippers, polytheists, or deviated from the religion of Monotheism; rather, "they know well [for whom they have permission to intercede]" (وَ هُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In this way, their hope for the intercession of angels is severed for two reasons: First, the angels themselves bear witness to the Oneness of Allah, and for this reason, they have gained permission to intercede. Second, they know well the proper place and subject worthy of intercession. [^1]

[^1]: According to this commentary, the exception "إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِ‌" is a "connected exception" (istithna' muttasil). However, if the phrase "الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ" refers specifically to idols, the exception becomes "disconnected" (istithna' munqati'). But the first meaning seems more appropriate, especially considering "الذين" which is used for rational beings, or as a generalization (taghlib) for both rational and non-rational beings.

Some have considered the phrase "وَ هُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ‌" as a complement to "إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِ‌," according to which the meaning of the sentence becomes: only those have the right of intercession who bear witness to Monotheism and are aware of its reality. However, the first commentary is more suitable.

In any case, this verse specifies the primary condition for intercessors in the presence of Allah: they are those who are witnesses to the truth, who recognize the truth in all stages, are well-aware of the spirit of Monotheism, and are also informed of the conditions of those seeking intercession.

Then, drawing from the beliefs of the polytheists themselves, He gives them a crushing response and says: "And if you ask them who created them, they will surely say: 'Allah'!" (وَ لَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ‌).

We have repeatedly stated that among the Arab and non-Arab polytheists, rarely was someone found who considered the idols to be the creators. Rather, they worshipped idols as intermediaries and intercessors before Allah, or as symbols and signs of the sacred existences of the Friends of Allah (Awliya' Allah), combined with the excuse that "our deity must be a tangible being so that we may feel close to it." Therefore, whenever they were asked about the Creator, they would identify the Creator as "Allah."

The Quran frequently reminds us of this reality: that worship is worthy only for the Creator and Disposer of this universe. Since you acknowledge Him as the Creator and Disposer, you have no choice but to consider servitude and divinity as exclusive to Him as well.

Thus, at the end of the verse, by way of blame and reproach, He says: Since this is the case, "how then are they turned away [from the worship of Allah toward others]"?! (فانى يؤفكون).

The next verse speaks of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله complaint before Allah regarding this stubborn and illogical people, saying: "How are they heedless of the Prophet's complaint in the presence of Allah when he says:

'O my Lord, indeed these are a people who do not believe'"? (وَ قِيلِهِ يا رَبِّ إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ قَوْمٌ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

He says: I spoke to them night and day, I approached them through glad tidings and warnings, I recounted the painful stories of previous nations to them, I threatened them with Your punishment, and encouraged them toward Your mercy if they returned from this deviant path. In short, I expressed whatever was in my power and said whatever needed to be said, but despite all this, my warm words did not affect their cold hearts and they did not believe. You know best, and so do they. [^1]

And in the final verse, He commands him: Since this is the case, turn away from them (فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ‌). At the same time, your turning away should not mean a break accompanied by violence or aggression.

[^1]: Regarding what "قيله" is coordinated (atf) with, there is much discussion among commentators. Some consider it coordinated with "الساعة" (the Hour) three verses prior; thus, the meaning becomes: "Allah is aware of the Resurrection and of the Prophet's complaint regarding the disbelievers." Others consider it coordinated with "علم الساعة" (knowledge of the Hour), with the implication that "knowledge" is an omitted prefix before "قيله," which does not differ much in meaning from the first interpretation. A group has considered the "waw" to be the "waw of oath," along with other possibilities that would be lengthy to recount. There is another possibility here that might be better than all that has been said: that it is coordinated with the omitted part of the sentence "انى يؤفكون," and the estimation becomes: "How are they turned away from His worship and how do they ignore the Prophet's complaint about this faithless people?"

Rather, "say to them: Peace" (وَ قُلْ سَلامٌ‌).

Not a peace of friendship and greeting, but a peace of parting and estrangement. This "Salam" is in fact similar to the "Salam" mentioned in verse 63 of Surah Al-Furqan: وَ إِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً: "And when the ignorant address them [with harsh words], they say, 'Peace'"—a peace that is a sign of indifference coupled with nobility.

Nevertheless, He threatens them with a meaningful sentence, so they do not imagine that this parting and farewell is proof that Allah has nothing to do with them. He says: "But they will soon know" (فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Yes, they will know what scorching fire and painful punishment they have prepared for themselves through their stubbornness! Some have mentioned a "reason for revelation" (shan nuzul) for the verse "وَ لا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ ...", which is that: "Nadr ibn al-Harith" and a few people from the Quraysh said: "If what Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله says is true, we have no need for his intercession; we love the angels and consider them our guardians, they are more worthy of intercession." It was then that the above verse was revealed (warning them that the angels do not intercede for anyone on the Day of Resurrection except for those who bear witness to the truth, meaning the believers). [^1] [^2] And here Surah Az-Zukhruf ends.

O Lord! Make our connection and bond with Yourself and Your Friends (Awliya') stronger day by day so that we may be included in their intercession.

O Allah! Keep us protected and apart from every kind of manifest and hidden polytheism.

O Lord! The Day of Resurrection, with the descriptions You have stated in Your Heavenly Book, is a difficult and grueling day. On that day, deal with us with Your Grace, not with Your Justice. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Az-Zukhruf. 3 Rajab 1405 / March 25, 1985.

[^1]: According to this commentary, the phrase "إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِ‌" is a description for those receiving intercession, not the intercessors.

[^2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 5942.
